proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Real-World Robustness: A Benchmark for Distribution Shift in Language Models
proposals.motivation: A core assumption in AI safety and deployment is that scaling models improves their robustness to real-world distribution shifts, such as novel user intents, evolving social norms, or adversarial inputs. However, pre-2026 evidence is mixed, with some studies showing scaling improves in-distribution but not out-of-distribution performance. If larger models are not more robust in practice, this challenges the safety case for scaling and necessitates new approaches to reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling model parameters does not reliably improve robustness to realistic, structured distribution shifts, and that performance may even plateau or degrade on certain high-stakes robustness categories.
proposals.method: We will construct a new benchmark, the Robustness Under Distribution Shift (RUDS) suite, comprising curated and synthetic datasets representing realistic shifts in style, reasoning, and factual knowledge. We will evaluate a controlled series of publicly available LLMs (e.g., from the GPT, LLaMA, and Claude families) on RUDS, analyzing performance trends against model scale. The analysis will use controlled scaling curves and disentangle the effects of scale from those of improved training data and alignment techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, empirical scaling law analysis focused on real-world robustness for language models. It will produce a public benchmark for tracking robustness and offer concrete recommendations for whether and how scaling should be coupled with other techniques to achieve trustworthy AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies scaling for in-distribution accuracy or narrow adversarial attacks, this proposal directly tests the scaling-robustness assumption for broad, realistic distribution shifts, a critical gap for safety and deployment.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Robustness Under Compound Distribution Shifts: A Framework for Evaluating LLM Trustworthiness
proposals.motivation: Current evaluations of LLM robustness and trustworthiness typically test against single, isolated distribution shifts (e.g., only adversarial prompts or only domain-specific data). This fails to model the real-world deployment environment where models face multiple, simultaneous shifts in input distribution, privacy constraints, and agent behavior. A comprehensive assessment of model safety requires a benchmark that combines these challenges to reveal failure modes that single-shift evaluations miss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that robustness methods (e.g., adversarial training, privacy-preserving fine-tuning, unlearning) evaluated solely on single distribution shifts will show significantly degraded performance when tested against concurrent, compound shifts, revealing critical gaps in current safety evaluations.
proposals.method: We will develop the Compound Shift Evaluation (CSE) framework, building on methodologies from Hendrycks' prior work on MMDT and WMDP. CSE will algorithmically generate and combine multiple pre-2026 shift types—including semantic adversarial attacks (inspired by jailbreaking), privacy leakage probes (extending LLM-PBE), and distributional drifts from agent interaction logs—into a single benchmark suite. We will systematically evaluate leading LLMs and robustness techniques across these compound scenarios, measuring performance degradation, privacy violations, and safety alignment failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first open-source benchmark for compound distribution shift evaluation in LLMs, providing a more realistic and stress-testing platform for trustworthiness research. It will yield concrete findings on which robustness methods fail under combined pressures, guiding the development of more holistic safety interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior benchmarks test shifts in isolation, our framework's core novelty is the systematic composition and concurrent application of multiple, distinct shift types—adversarial, privacy, and agent-based—to simulate complex real-world failure modes, moving beyond the single-axis evaluation paradigm.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Augmentation-Induced Robustness: A Benchmark for Trustworthy LLMs
proposals.motivation: The scaling hypothesis has driven LLM capabilities, but its interaction with data augmentation for robustness remains poorly understood. Pre-2026, scaling often improves performance on clean data, yet it is unclear if it consistently enhances robustness to adversarial inputs, distribution shifts, and privacy attacks. A systematic study is needed to guide the development of models that are both capable and trustworthy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relationship between model scale and robustness is non-monotonic and task-dependent, and that targeted data augmentation can create synergistic effects with scaling, disproportionately improving robustness in certain threat models compared to scaling alone.
proposals.method: We will construct a new benchmark, the Scaling-Augmentation Robustness Profile (SARP), integrating diverse threat models from prior work (e.g., adversarial suffixes, stylistic shifts, privacy extraction). We will train a family of decoder-only LLMs (e.g., 100M to 10B parameters) on the Pile, applying systematic augmentation techniques (e.g., back-translation, counterfactual augmentation, controlled noise injection). We will analyze scaling curves for both clean and robust performance, using statistical methods to identify inflection points and measure the marginal robustness gain per parameter under different augmentations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) SARP, a public benchmark for evaluating scale-augmentation interactions, 2) empirical scaling laws for multiple robustness dimensions, and 3) practical guidelines on whether and when to invest in scaling vs. augmentation for safety. This directly advances the field of trustworthy AI evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While scaling and augmentation have been studied in isolation, their interaction is not characterized. This proposal uniquely frames robustness as a function of both variables, moving beyond the assumption that 'scale alone solves robustness' to provide a nuanced, evidence-based perspective.

proposals.title: AugMix for Trustworthy Multimodal Models: Extending Robustness Enhancement to Audio and Text Modalities
proposals.motivation: The AugMix method has proven highly effective for improving model robustness and uncertainty calibration in computer vision, a critical component of trustworthy AI. As foundation models become increasingly multimodal, there is a pressing need to develop and evaluate analogous data augmentation strategies for other core modalities like audio and text to ensure their reliability and safety. This work directly addresses the trustworthiness evaluation gap for non-vision modalities highlighted in recent research, such as MMDT.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core principles of AugMix—specifically, the application of diverse, compositionally invariant augmentations within a Jensen-Shannon Divergence consistency loss framework—can be successfully adapted to audio and text data, leading to significant improvements in model robustness, calibration, and out-of-distribution generalization for these modalities.
proposals.method: We will design modality-specific augmentation chains: for audio, using time-stretching, pitch-shifting, and background noise mixing; for text, employing synonym replacement, syntactic tree manipulations, and controlled paraphrasing. These chains will be integrated into the AugMix pipeline, generating multiple augmented views. We will train models using the original AugMix consistency loss, enforcing prediction invariance across these views. Evaluation will be conducted on established robustness benchmarks for audio (e.g., Speech Commands under noise) and text (e.g., adversarial NLI datasets), measuring standard accuracy, calibration error, and OOD detection performance.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver the first systematic adaptation of AugMix to audio and text, providing open-source implementations and new benchmarks for multimodal robustness. It will contribute directly to the field of AI safety by offering a practical, training-time method to enhance the trustworthiness of language and audio models, complementing post-hoc evaluation and benchmarking efforts. The findings will inform the development of more reliable multimodal agents.
proposals.novelty: While AugMix is established for images, its principled application to sequential, structured data like audio and text is unexplored. The novelty lies in designing semantically meaningful, label-preserving augmentation chains for these domains and rigorously testing the transferability of the AugMix consistency framework, moving beyond simple additive noise or basic synonym replacement.

proposals.title: Adversarial Curriculum Learning for Robust Code Generation: A Closed-Loop Benchmarking and Training Framework
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for evaluating and training LLMs on coding tasks, like HumanEval, rely on static datasets. This leads to models that overfit to known problem patterns, failing on novel or adversarially constructed challenges, which is a critical trustworthiness and safety gap for real-world deployment. A dynamic, adversarial evaluation and training paradigm is needed to systematically probe and improve model robustness, moving beyond static benchmarks.
proposals.hypothesis: A closed-loop adversarial curriculum, where a generator model synthesizes novel coding problems targeting a learner model's specific failure modes, will produce a more robust and generalizable coding model than one trained on static datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop a three-agent system: a Generator (e.g., a fine-tuned LLM) creates novel coding problems; a Learner (the target model) solves them; and an Evaluator (e.g., a fine-tuned LLM or verifier) assesses correctness and classifies error types (e.g., logical, algorithmic, API misuse). The Evaluator's feedback on failure modes will be used to prompt the Generator, creating a targeted adversarial curriculum. We will adapt and extend pre-2026 methods from WMDP (for targeted challenge generation) and MMDT (for multi-faceted evaluation) to the coding domain.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, automated framework for generating adaptive, adversarial coding benchmarks. 2) Empirical evidence on whether adversarial curricula improve robustness beyond static data training. 3) A new public benchmark dataset of adversarially generated coding challenges, extending the lineage of safety-focused benchmarks from Hendrycks' prior work.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and data augmentation exist, applying a closed-loop, curriculum-based adversarial generation system specifically to code—where the generator's objective is to exploit *conceptual* weaknesses identified by an evaluator—is a novel synthesis of robustness evaluation and automated training.

proposals.title: ACL-Code: Adversarial Curriculum Learning for Robust Coding Competence via Synthetic Problem Generation
proposals.motivation: Current LLMs for code are trained on static datasets of coding problems, which often have coverage gaps and fail to systematically test rare or challenging algorithmic concepts. This leads to brittle performance and poor generalization on novel, out-of-distribution coding challenges, a critical trustworthiness and safety concern for AI coding assistants. Improving robustness requires methods to generate diverse, high-quality, and valid training data that targets these weaknesses.
proposals.hypothesis: An adversarial curriculum learning framework, adapted from ecological niche modeling, can generate synthetically valid and diverse coding problems that, when used for training, will significantly improve LLM robustness and generalization on coding benchmarks, especially for underrepresented problem types.
proposals.method: We will model the feature space of human-authored coding problems (e.g., from APPS or HumanEval) using metadata tags, solution code embeddings, and complexity metrics. A generator network (e.g., a fine-tuned LLM) will propose novel problem statements and specifications. A discriminator network, trained to distinguish real from synthetic problems, will enforce realism and solvability. The curriculum will be controlled by progressively expanding the 'envelope' of valid feature combinations, prioritizing the generation of problems in low-density regions of the training data distribution, analogous to generating plausible species habitats.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel framework for controllable, high-fidelity synthetic data augmentation tailored to code. It will yield a new benchmark, ACL-Code, for evaluating generalization on synthetically generated edge-case problems. The results will provide empirical evidence on how targeted data augmentation impacts robustness in code LLMs, contributing to the broader field of AI safety and evaluation.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting adversarial curriculum learning and ecological niche modeling concepts to the structured domain of code generation. Unlike simple data mixing or back-translation, it explicitly targets the generative modeling of the *problem specification space* to address coverage gaps systematically.

proposals.title: The CodeTrust Benchmark: A Trustworthiness-Focused Dataset for Evaluating and Improving Code Generation
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for code generation, like HumanEval, primarily measure functional correctness but neglect critical trustworthiness dimensions such as security, privacy, and robustness. As LLMs are increasingly integrated into software development pipelines, these models must not only be competent but also safe and reliable. A lack of high-quality, diverse training and evaluation data targeting these properties hinders progress in developing trustworthy coding assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that curating a benchmark dataset with explicit annotations for security vulnerabilities, privacy leaks, and robustness failures in code will enable more targeted training and fine-tuning, leading to language models that generate more trustworthy code without sacrificing general coding competence.
proposals.method: We will construct the CodeTrust benchmark by: 1) Curating a diverse set of coding problems from existing sources (e.g., LeetCode, CWE lists) and generating synthetic variants. 2) Annotating each problem and its solutions with multi-dimensional trustworthiness labels (e.g., vulnerability type, privacy risk) using a combination of static analysis tools and expert review. 3) Developing a fine-tuning and evaluation protocol that uses this dataset to train models with a multi-objective loss balancing correctness and safety, potentially using unlearning techniques inspired by WMDP to reduce harmful capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, multi-dimensional trustworthiness benchmark for code generation. It will also deliver a methodology and open-source toolkit for generating safety-annotated coding data and demonstrate that targeted training on such data measurably improves model trustworthiness on held-out evaluations.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior coding benchmarks focused on correctness, CodeTrust explicitly targets the safety and security dimensions of code, aligning with Hendrycks' research on AI risk. The integration of data curation with unlearning-based training methods for code-specific risks is a novel application of pre-2026 safety techniques to the programming domain.

proposals.title: The CodeTrust-Eval Benchmark: Assessing Competence and Risk in Small-Scale Code Generation Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models for code (e.g., Codex, CodeLlama) are computationally expensive and opaque, raising deployment costs and trustworthiness concerns. There is a pressing need for smaller, more efficient models that retain competent code generation abilities, but existing benchmarks (e.g., HumanEval) primarily measure capability, not the safety and reliability risks that become critical for widespread, efficient deployment. This gap hinders the trustworthy development of efficient coding assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying targeted architectural innovations and rigorous multi-dimensional evaluation, we can develop sub-10B parameter models that achieve competent performance on standard code generation tasks while exhibiting measurably lower risks related to code security, privacy, and malicious use compared to their larger counterparts.
proposals.method: We will develop a suite of sub-10B parameter models using a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture and structured knowledge distillation from a large teacher model (e.g., CodeLlama 34B). We will then construct the CodeTrust-Eval benchmark, extending HumanEval with novel test suites inspired by WMDP and LLM-PBE methodologies to assess risks like insecure code generation, privacy leakage in code comments, and susceptibility to generating malicious scripts. Evaluation will measure the trade-off between efficiency (FLOPs, latency), competence (pass@k), and trustworthiness scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, publicly available benchmark for evaluating code generation models on competence and risk dimensions, and 2) a family of efficient, open-source model architectures with documented trustworthiness profiles. This provides a framework for the safer and more efficient development of coding assistants.
proposals.novelty: While model compression for code exists, our approach uniquely integrates a Hendrycks-style multi-dimensional risk assessment directly into the development and evaluation cycle of small code models, creating a benchmark that explicitly ties efficiency to trustworthiness, not just capability.

proposals.title: Robust Outlier Exposure: Training for OOD Detection with Contaminated Auxiliary Data
proposals.motivation: Outlier Exposure (OE) is a leading method for improving out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in deep neural networks, but it assumes access to a clean, diverse set of outlier data, which is often impractical. In real-world safety-critical applications for LLMs and vision models, available auxiliary datasets are often contaminated with in-distribution samples or have only weak, noisy labels. This gap limits the practical deployment of OE for enhancing model trustworthiness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training framework which explicitly models and accounts for contamination within the auxiliary dataset can recover robust OOD detection performance, outperforming naive OE and matching the performance of OE with a perfectly clean outlier set.
proposals.method: We propose a two-pronged approach: 1) Develop a contamination-robust loss function, building on ideas from noise-robust classification (e.g., Generalized Cross Entropy), that down-weights the influence of samples likely to be in-distribution contaminants. 2) Integrate a lightweight, concurrently trained discriminator module that estimates the probability a given auxiliary sample is a true outlier, using distributional statistics from the primary training set. This probability will gate the contribution of each auxiliary sample to the OE loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic method for applying OE with realistic, messy data, directly increasing its practical utility for safety evaluation. We will release a new benchmark for evaluating OOD detection methods under varying levels of auxiliary data contamination, extending existing trustworthiness benchmarks like those from Hendrycks et al.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses clean OE or focuses on synthetic noise, we explicitly address the realistic scenario of unknown in-distribution contamination within the auxiliary outlier set. Our method's joint estimation of contamination and OOD detection is a novel combination of ideas from robust learning and density estimation.

proposals.title: OASIS: Optimized Auxiliary Selection for In-distribution Safety in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current methods for evaluating and improving the trustworthiness of LLMs, such as out-of-distribution (OOD) detection and adversarial robustness, rely on manually curated auxiliary datasets. This selection is ad-hoc, leading to inconsistent and non-generalizable safety assessments. A systematic, automated approach is needed to ensure evaluations are both rigorous and efficient, directly addressing core safety risks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an automated, task-aware method for selecting auxiliary outlier datasets will produce more reliable and sensitive trustworthiness evaluations (e.g., for OOD detection, privacy leakage, or harmful content generation) than current heuristic selection, leading to stronger model safety interventions.
proposals.method: We will frame auxiliary dataset selection as an optimization problem. For a given in-distribution (ID) safety task (e.g., the WMDP benchmark), we will use pre-2026 representation learning techniques (e.g., contrastive learning with BERT or CLIP embeddings) to compute a 'distributional tension' score between candidate auxiliary datasets and the ID data. We will then train a lightweight meta-selector model, using techniques from meta-learning (e.g., MAML) or Bayesian optimization, to predict which auxiliary sets maximize the performance of a downstream safety probe (e.g., an OOD detector or privacy auditor) on held-out ID validation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled framework for auxiliary dataset selection in model safety, moving beyond manual curation. We will release a benchmark suite and an open-source toolkit for optimized selection, enabling more standardized and effective evaluations of LLM trustworthiness across domains like malicious use, privacy, and robustness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., Hendrycks et al.'s benchmarks) creates static auxiliary datasets, this project dynamically *selects* them. It formalizes the connection between auxiliary data characteristics and safety probe efficacy, a relationship previously only understood intuitively.

proposals.title: AdvOE: Adversarial Outlier Exposure for Robust Uncertainty Quantification in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Outlier Exposure (OE) is a promising method for improving uncertainty estimation in language models by exposing them to auxiliary outlier data. However, its effectiveness is bottlenecked by the availability and diversity of natural outlier datasets. This limits the model's ability to recognize challenging, adversarially crafted inputs that lie near or outside its training distribution, a critical weakness for safety-critical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that generating synthetic outliers via targeted adversarial attacks on a model's confidence will create a more challenging and informative OE dataset, leading to significantly improved calibration and robustness in out-of-distribution (OOD) detection compared to OE with natural outliers.
proposals.method: We will adapt Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) and AutoAttack frameworks to generate adversarial outliers. The attack objective will be to maximize the model's confidence (i.e., minimize predictive entropy) for inputs that are semantically or syntactically OOD. We will generate these adversarial examples from a held-out, non-overlapping corpus (e.g., Wikipedia for a scientific model) and iteratively expose the model to them during fine-tuning. The approach will be evaluated on established OOD detection benchmarks (e.g., CLINC-OOS, ANLI) using metrics like AUROC and expected calibration error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, automated method for creating high-quality OE data, directly addressing a key practical limitation. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating OOD robustness under adversarial distributional shift and offer concrete techniques to harden language models against spurious confidence on unfamiliar inputs.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is well-studied for robustness to perturbations, applying its principles specifically to craft *distributional* outliers for OE is novel. This bridges the fields of adversarial robustness and uncertainty estimation in a targeted way not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Meta-Outlier Exposure: Fast Adaptation of Out-of-Distribution Detectors via Gradient-Based Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in dynamic, open-world settings, they encounter novel, potentially harmful inputs not seen during training. Current Outlier Exposure (OE) methods for OOD detection require extensive, static datasets and costly retraining for each new threat domain, limiting their practical utility for rapid response to emerging risks. A method for fast, few-shot adaptation of anomaly detectors is critical for trustworthy and safe AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model meta-trained on a distribution of OE tasks can rapidly adapt its anomaly detection head to new, unseen outlier types with minimal exposure, significantly outperforming standard fine-tuning approaches in few-shot settings.
proposals.method: We will employ a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework. A base model (e.g., a pretrained LLM encoder) will be meta-trained on a constructed suite of OE tasks, where each task defines a different 'in-distribution' (e.g., a specific topic or benign dataset) and a held-out 'outlier' class. The inner-loop adaptation will fine-tune a lightweight anomaly scoring head (e.g., a linear probe on CLS token) using a few OE examples, and the outer loop will update the base model's parameters to enable fast adaptation. We will benchmark against standard OE and fine-tuning baselines on curated risk benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical framework for rapidly hardening LLMs against evolving threat models, directly advancing the field of AI safety and trustworthiness. It will also provide a new benchmark for evaluating few-shot OOD adaptation, extending the scope of evaluations like Hendrycks' prior work on WMDP and MMDT.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been used for few-shot classification, its application to rapidly adapt *anomaly detection* and OOD scoring mechanisms for LLMs, specifically within the OE paradigm, is a novel and unexplored direction for improving model safety agility.

proposals.title: MM-OE: A Massively Multi-Task Outlier Exposure Framework for Robust and Trustworthy Language Models
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in increasingly diverse and high-stakes domains, their trustworthiness hinges on reliably detecting and rejecting out-of-distribution (OOD) or anomalous inputs. Current Outlier Exposure (OE) methods are limited, typically training on a single, narrow outlier dataset, which fails to capture the vast, multi-faceted nature of real-world anomalies. This creates brittle safety filters that can be evaded by inputs from an unexposed domain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a single model on a curated, massive corpus of outliers spanning hundreds of distinct domains (e.g., code, medicine, finance, adversarial prompts, non-English languages) will yield a significantly more robust and generalizable OOD detector, improving performance on unseen anomaly types without catastrophic forgetting of in-distribution tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct the MM-OE dataset by aggregating and deduplicating existing OOD/negative datasets from NLP, security (e.g., WMDP), privacy (e.g., PII corpora), and synthetic adversarial generations. We will fine-tune a pre-trained language model (e.g., Llama 2) using a contrastive or margin-based loss (e.g., a modified CLIP-style objective) to simultaneously separate in-distribution examples from all outlier domains. The architecture will include a domain-agnostic projection head to produce a unified 'trustworthiness' score, evaluated rigorously on a held-out suite of domain-specific OOD benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) the first large-scale, multi-domain benchmark for evaluating OOD detection generality in LLMs, 2) a publicly released model and methodology for Massively Multi-Task OE that sets a new standard for anomaly detection robustness, and 3) analysis of transfer and interference effects between outlier domains during training.
proposals.novelty: While OE is established, scaling it to hundreds of concurrent, diverse outlier tasks is unexplored and non-trivial, moving beyond single-domain fine-tuning to create a unified 'anomaly immune system' for LLMs. This directly extends Hendrycks' prior work on benchmarks (MMDT, WMDP) and trustworthiness into a novel, integrated training paradigm.

proposals.title: Adversarial Filtration: A Causal Audit of Feature Degradation in Robust Language Models
proposals.motivation: Adversarial filtration is a key pre-training data curation technique, used to remove 'shortcuts' and improve model robustness and trustworthiness. However, its core assumption—that it removes only spurious cues—remains untested. If filtration inadvertently degrades core semantic features, it could systematically harm model capabilities and reliability, undermining safety efforts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adversarial filtration, while removing spurious cues, also causally degrades a model's representation of core, task-relevant features, leading to fragile and miscalibrated trust in the model's outputs.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the WMDP Benchmark's unlearning evaluation framework. For a set of core concepts (e.g., scientific facts, ethical principles), we will construct paired datasets: one with spurious cues and one without. Using causal mediation analysis and feature attribution methods (e.g., TracIn, linear probes), we will measure the change in the strength and accessibility of core feature representations in models trained on filtered vs. unfiltered data. We will quantify downstream impacts on calibration and robustness using Hendrycks' established evaluation suites (e.g., MMD, AI Safety Benchmark).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal audit of adversarial filtration's side effects, moving beyond performance metrics to explain *why* filtered models fail. It will yield practical guidelines for safer data curation and contribute a new diagnostic benchmark for evaluating feature preservation in trustworthy AI pipelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work measures performance after filtration, we directly test its foundational assumption by causally analyzing feature-level changes, bridging data curation research with mechanistic interpretability.

proposals.title: Benchmarking the Benchmarks: A Stress-Test of ImageNet's Real-World Generalization Claims
proposals.motivation: The ImageNet test set has become a de facto standard for measuring progress in computer vision. However, its role as a sufficient proxy for real-world robustness and generalization is largely assumed, not rigorously validated. This creates a critical trustworthiness gap: models that excel on this static benchmark may fail unpredictably in practical applications, posing risks for safety-critical deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ImageNet test set is an insufficient benchmark for real-world generalization, and that models achieving high ImageNet accuracy will exhibit significant, systematic performance degradation under a structured suite of distribution shifts and adversarial conditions not represented in the standard evaluation.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-faceted evaluation suite, 'ImageNet-RW' (Real-World), extending beyond standard corruptions. This includes: 1) Natural distribution shifts using datasets like ImageNet-V2 and ImageNet-Sketch; 2) Privacy-focused adversarial examples generated via PGD attacks to test memorization and leakage; 3) Compositional reasoning tasks requiring models to combine concepts from the training data in novel ways. We will evaluate a diverse set of pre-trained models (e.g., ResNets, Vision Transformers) on this suite, correlating performance drops with architectural features and training procedures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, evidence-based assessment of a foundational benchmark's limitations, moving the field beyond a single-number metric. It will yield a new diagnostic suite for model trustworthiness and establish clearer guidelines for what claims can be responsibly made from ImageNet performance alone.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied specific distribution shifts, our approach uniquely integrates privacy attacks and compositional generalization into a holistic benchmark stress-test, directly challenging the sufficiency claim of the standard test set from a trustworthiness and safety perspective.

proposals.title: The Universal Image Benchmark (UIB): Synthesizing and Curating Foolproof Visual Concepts for Robust Model Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Research on adversarial examples and model failures has focused on finding weaknesses, but this provides an incomplete picture of trustworthiness. A critical, complementary problem is identifying visual concepts that are universally and robustly understood by diverse models. Such 'foolproof' images would establish a high-confidence baseline for safe deployment, crucial for high-stakes applications like medical imaging or autonomous systems where consistent interpretation is non-negotiable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, optimization-based search over image and concept space can identify a core set of visual stimuli that elicit high-agreement, correct classifications across a wide distribution of state-of-the-art vision and multimodal models, revealing fundamental shared representations.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, a generative stage will use evolutionary algorithms or gradient-based optimization on diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) to synthesize images that maximize consensus logits for a target class across a panel of models (CLIP, DINOv2, ResNets). Second, a large-scale curation stage will filter massive datasets (e.g., LAION) using a novel 'agreement score' metric, selecting naturally occurring high-agreement examples. We will evaluate on the WMDP-inspired panel of models, measuring agreement variance and robustness to perturbations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first public benchmark of 'foolproof' images, providing a necessary tool for stress-testing model consistency and foundational visual understanding. It will also yield insights into the shared inductive biases of modern vision architectures, informing more robust and aligned model design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (like MMDT) evaluates trustworthiness by probing for failures, we invert the objective to proactively construct a high-reliability benchmark. This shifts the paradigm from vulnerability discovery to establishing a positive, consensus-driven standard for model safety.

proposals.title: FINE-GRAINED ADVERSARIAL SUBCATEGORIES (FAS): A METHOD FOR IDENTIFYING LATENT FAILURE MODES IN TRUSTWORTHY AI BENCHMARKS
proposals.motivation: Current AI safety and trustworthiness benchmarks often treat broad categories (e.g., 'harmful content', 'privacy leakage') as monolithic, potentially masking significant performance variations on challenging subcategories. This coarse-grained evaluation creates a false sense of security, as models may appear robust while failing systematically on specific, fine-grained adversarial examples within a class. Identifying these latent failure modes is critical for developing more reliable and trustworthy models.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic filtration and clustering of model failures within a single benchmark class will reveal semantically coherent, fine-grained subcategories that are disproportionately challenging, and these subcategories represent generalizable, latent adversarial weaknesses not captured by aggregate scores.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage method. First, for a target class (e.g., 'dangerous knowledge' from WMDP), we use an LLM-based adversarial generator to produce a large, diverse set of in-class examples. Second, we apply iterative filtration: we evaluate a suite of frontier models on this set, cluster the persistent failures using embedding-based semantic clustering (e.g., via Sentence-BERT), and use human-in-the-loop analysis to label the resulting subcategories. The final output is the FAS dataset, comprising identified subcategories and their adversarial examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodology and public dataset for fine-grained adversarial evaluation, moving beyond monolithic class scores. It will provide actionable insights for AI developers by pinpointing specific, coherent failure modes, thereby enabling targeted robustness improvements. This contributes directly to the fields of AI safety and evaluation by making trustworthiness assessments more precise and interpretable.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates adversarial examples *across* classes or for overall score reduction, our approach systematically discovers and characterizes the *internal* structure of failure within a single benchmark class, linking clusters of failures to semantically meaningful subcategories.

proposals.title: CRaFT: A Benchmark and Framework for Corruption Robustness in Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: As foundation models are deployed in real-world, high-stakes applications, their performance must remain reliable under non-ideal conditions, such as corrupted inputs, adversarial perturbations, or distribution shifts. Pre-2026 robustness research has focused heavily on vision models (e.g., ImageNet-C) and adversarial attacks on LLMs, but lacks a systematic, multi-modal framework for evaluating and improving corruption robustness across text, code, and reasoning tasks. This gap directly impacts the trustworthiness and safety of these systems in practical use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, multi-modal corruption benchmark will reveal critical, task-specific failure modes in foundation models, and that targeted robustness interventions—inspired by data augmentation and adversarial training—can be developed to significantly mitigate these vulnerabilities without sacrificing standard performance.
proposals.method: We will first develop the CRaFT (Corruption Robustness for Foundation Tasks) benchmark, extending principles from ImageNet-C and text perturbation sets to create a unified suite of realistic corruptions (e.g., typos, semantic noise, code obfuscation, prompt injections) across diverse modalities and tasks. We will then evaluate a range of pre-2026 robustness methods, including data augmentation with corrupted samples, adversarial training with a focus on gradient-based perturbation, and inference-time filtering. The core technical approach will involve adapting and scaling these methods, particularly adversarial training with projected gradient descent and consistency regularization, for large-scale language and multimodal models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, multi-modal benchmark for corruption robustness in foundation models, establishing a new standard for evaluation. It will deliver a publicly available framework and a set of empirically validated methods for enhancing robustness, directly contributing to the safety and reliability of AI systems. The findings will inform future research on model hardening and trustworthiness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied robustness in isolation (e.g., adversarial text or image corruptions), CRaFT integrates a systematic taxonomy of corruptions across modalities into a single benchmark. Furthermore, it moves beyond mere evaluation to systematically test and adapt pre-2026 robustness-enhancing algorithms at the scale of modern foundation models, a largely unexplored area.

proposals.title: Aligning Distributions for Trustworthy Vision: Domain Adaptation for Corruption Robustness in Multimodal Models
proposals.motivation: The trustworthiness and safety of multimodal foundation models (MFMs) are critical for real-world deployment, where input data is often corrupted by noise, weather, or sensor degradation. Pre-2026 benchmarks like ImageNet-C reveal a significant performance drop in vision models under such corruptions, posing a major reliability risk. This work directly addresses the 'robustness' pillar of trustworthiness evaluation, a core theme in Hendrycks' research, by moving beyond mere measurement to active improvement.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) techniques to align the feature distributions of clean and corrupted images will significantly improve the corruption robustness of vision encoders within MFMs, thereby enhancing their overall trustworthiness.
proposals.method: We will adapt and evaluate established UDA algorithms, specifically Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks (DANN) and Contrastive Adaptation Network (CAN), to learn domain-invariant features between clean (source) and corrupted (target) image domains. The target corruptions will be defined by standard benchmarks like ImageNet-C and our proposed extensions. We will integrate these adapted encoders into open-source MFMs (e.g., OpenFlamingo) and evaluate robustness gains on a new benchmark suite measuring accuracy under corruption, alongside established trustworthiness metrics for consistency and fairness.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce: 1) a novel methodology for building corruption-robust MFMs via domain adaptation, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating corruption robustness in a multimodal context, extending work like MMDT, and 3) open-source adapted model weights. This contributes directly to the field of AI safety by providing a concrete technique to improve model reliability against distributional shifts.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is well-studied for natural domain shifts, its systematic application to *synthetic corruptions* for the explicit purpose of improving AI safety and trustworthiness in MFMs is novel. This bridges Hendrycks' prior work on robustness benchmarks with proactive mitigation strategies, distinct from post-hoc correction or simple data augmentation.

proposals.title: SimCorrupt: A Graphics-Engine-Based Benchmark for Robustness Evaluation of Multimodal Models
proposals.motivation: Current robustness benchmarks for vision and multimodal models often rely on simplistic, hand-crafted image corruptions (e.g., Gaussian noise, blur) that fail to capture the complex, structured, and realistic distribution shifts encountered in real-world deployment, such as adverse weather, sensor degradation, or unusual lighting. This gap limits our ability to accurately assess and improve model trustworthiness and safety, a core concern in AI alignment research. A more systematic, physically-grounded approach to generating diverse corruptions is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using modern, realistic graphics simulators (e.g., Unreal Engine, NVIDIA Omniverse) to generate a diverse spectrum of structured visual corruptions will create a more challenging and ecologically valid robustness benchmark, revealing critical failure modes in multimodal foundation models that current benchmarks miss.
proposals.method: We will develop SimCorrupt, a pipeline that uses a graphics engine to apply parameterized, realistic corruptions (e.g., fog, rain, lens flare, motion blur, time-of-day lighting) to clean images from existing vision datasets. We will define a taxonomy of corruption families based on real-world failure modes and sample parameters from realistic distributions. We will then evaluate state-of-the-art multimodal models (e.g., LLaVA, Flamingo, GPT-4V) on this benchmark, measuring performance degradation across tasks like VQA and captioning. The benchmark will be integrated into the existing evaluation frameworks from the MMDT paper.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new, open-source benchmark of realistic, simulator-generated corruptions for multimodal robustness evaluation, 2) a systematic analysis of how state-of-the-art models fail under structured distribution shifts, and 3) a methodology for generating future robustness tests grounded in graphics simulation, advancing the field of AI safety evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation from simulation is common in training, its systematic application to create a comprehensive *evaluation* benchmark for multimodal model robustness is novel. This moves beyond simple pixel-level perturbations to structured, physically-plausible corruptions generated programmatically at scale.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Robustness Scoring (UARS): A Framework for Quantifying LLM Reliability Under Distribution Shift
proposals.motivation: Current robustness benchmarks for LLMs primarily measure performance degradation on out-of-distribution (OOD) data but fail to assess whether models can self-diagnose their own failures. This is a critical gap for trustworthiness, as a model that is both robust and calibrated in its uncertainty is safer for high-stakes deployment. Pre-2026, uncertainty quantification methods like Monte Carlo Dropout and Deep Ensembles are established in computer vision but under-explored for holistic LLM robustness evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating predictive uncertainty measures into robustness evaluation will produce a more informative reliability score, where models penalized for being confidently wrong on OOD data are better distinguished from those that appropriately express uncertainty when facing unfamiliar inputs.
proposals.method: We will extend existing robustness benchmarks (e.g., AdvGLUE, ANLI) by integrating an uncertainty quantification layer. For a given test suite, we will compute a composite UARS metric combining traditional accuracy with a penalty term derived from the divergence between predictive entropy and correctness. We will implement and compare multiple uncertainty estimators suitable for autoregressive LLMs, including ensembling techniques, sequence-level likelihood variance, and parametric confidence head fine-tuning. The framework will be tested across a range of foundation models to establish baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated benchmark and scoring methodology that jointly evaluates LLM performance and predictive uncertainty under distribution shift. It will deliver a public toolkit for uncertainty-aware robustness assessment and establish new leaderboards to incentivize the development of more reliable and self-aware models.
proposals.novelty: While uncertainty quantification and robustness are studied separately, their integration into a single, interpretable robustness score for LLMs is novel. This moves beyond merely detecting failures to evaluating a model's capacity for epistemic humility, a key dimension of trustworthiness not captured by prior benchmarks.

proposals.title: DualBench: Inverting Evaluation to Discover Adversarial Common Corruptions for Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Current robustness benchmarks for vision and language models rely on a fixed, handcrafted set of common corruptions (e.g., ImageNet-C). This provides a limited view of model vulnerabilities, potentially missing failure modes that adversaries could exploit. A systematic method to generate the most confusing, semantically plausible corruptions is needed to stress-test model trustworthiness more thoroughly.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the benchmark creation process and using gradient-based or evolutionary search, we can generate common corruptions that degrade model performance more severely than existing sets, revealing novel and critical robustness blind spots.
proposals.method: We will develop DUAL (Discovering Universal Adversarial Lapses), a framework that treats corruption generation as an optimization problem. For a target model (e.g., a multimodal or vision-language model), we will parameterize a space of plausible image and text corruptions (e.g., noise, blur, word substitutions). Using a two-stage approach—first, a gradient-based attack (like PGD) on a differentiable corruption layer, and second, a quality-diversity evolutionary algorithm—we will search for corruptions that maximize loss across a held-out dataset while maintaining perceptual similarity to natural inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new benchmark, DualBench, consisting of high-severity common corruptions. It will provide a more rigorous stress test for model robustness and offer diagnostic insights into specific architectural weaknesses. The inversion methodology will also establish a new paradigm for proactive, adversarial evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates corruptions to simulate natural variation, we explicitly optimize for maximal performance degradation within the constraints of common corruption, inverting the traditional benchmark goal. This connects adversarial robustness and common corruption research in a novel way.

proposals.title: MM-Corrupt: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Corruption Robustness of Multimodal Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Multimodal foundation models (e.g., CLIP, Flamingo, GPT-4V) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes, real-world environments where input data is often corrupted (e.g., sensor noise, motion blur, compression artifacts, or adversarial text perturbations). Pre-2026 research has extensively studied robustness in unimodal settings, but the vulnerability of multimodal systems to realistic, coordinated corruptions across modalities remains a critical, unassessed safety and trustworthiness gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art multimodal models exhibit significant and systematic degradation in performance under realistic, multi-modal corruptions, and that this robustness is not strongly correlated with performance on clean benchmarks, revealing a distinct failure mode.
proposals.method: We will develop the MM-Corrupt benchmark, extending established image corruption techniques (e.g., from ImageNet-C) and text perturbation methods (e.g., character swaps, synonym substitutions, stylistic changes) to multimodal tasks like VQA and image captioning. The core innovation is the systematic generation of *coordinated* corruptions, where perturbations in one modality (e.g., a blurry image) are semantically aligned or misaligned with perturbations in the other (e.g., a caption describing blur). We will evaluate a suite of models (open- and closed-source) using this benchmark and analyze failure patterns using influence functions and gradient-based attribution to identify architectural weak points.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, open-source benchmark for multi-modal corruption robustness, establishing a new standard for evaluating model trustworthiness in noisy environments. It will produce a diagnostic toolkit and analysis revealing which model architectures and training strategies confer greater robustness, directly informing safer model development.
proposals.novelty: While unimodal corruption benchmarks exist, MM-Corrupt is novel in its focus on *coordinated cross-modal corruptions*, which model real-world degradation scenarios, and its systematic evaluation of how corruption in one modality affects the interpretation of another.

proposals.title: RepE-MM: Scaling Representation Engineering for Trustworthy Multimodal Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: As multimodal foundation models (MFMs) scale in size and capability, their internal representations encode complex, often undesirable, concepts like bias, private data, and harmful intents. Pre-2026 work on Representation Engineering (RepE) has shown promise for controlling text-only LLMs via linear probes, but these techniques are untested on the massive, heterogeneous representations of models like GPT-4V or DALL-E 3. A scalable, modality-agnostic RepE framework is critical for the real-time safety and privacy evaluation of next-generation AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That linear concept subspaces can be robustly identified and manipulated within the fused representations of large multimodal models, enabling efficient, post-hoc control over safety-critical and privacy-related attributes without full model retraining.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale RepE techniques by: 1) Developing a benchmark suite of multimodal probes for concepts like privacy leakage (inspired by LLM-PBE), malicious use (extending WMDP), and trustworthiness (from MMDT). 2) Designing a novel, parameter-efficient cross-modal adapter to project image, audio, and text embeddings into a unified representation space where linear concept vectors are learned. 3) Implementing and evaluating control mechanisms—including activation steering and suppression—on open-source MFMs (e.g., LLaVA, OpenFlamingo) and, via API, on closed models, measuring intervention efficacy and task fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first scalable toolkit for representation monitoring and control in multimodal models, directly extending Hendrycks' line of safety evaluation research. It will yield a public benchmark for multimodal concept probing and demonstrate practical interventions to reduce risks, contributing to the mechanistic interpretability and alignment of large, black-box AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While RepE has been applied to LLMs, scaling it to the non-linear, high-dimensional latent spaces of multimodal models is an unsolved challenge. Our approach is novel in its focus on cross-modal concept consistency and its integration with established pre-2026 safety benchmarks (WMDP, MMDT) for evaluation.

proposals.title: Dual-Use Representations: Leveraging Internal State Monitoring for Efficient and Aligned Model Training
proposals.motivation: Current methods for improving model alignment (e.g., RLHF) and efficiency (e.g., pruning) are often costly, post-hoc, and operate on model outputs rather than internal mechanisms. As models scale, there is a critical need for more direct, interpretable, and computationally efficient training signals that can shape model behavior from within. This research addresses the dual challenge of enhancing both trustworthiness and efficiency, core themes in pre-2026 AI safety research.
proposals.hypothesis: That real-time monitoring and penalization of undesirable internal representations (e.g., those correlated with privacy leakage, harmful intent, or computational redundancy) during training can produce models that are simultaneously more aligned and more parameter-efficient than models trained with standard loss functions alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a training framework that augments standard loss with a 'representation loss'. This involves: 1) Defining and auditing sensitive or inefficient representation subspaces using probes or PCA on data from benchmarks like WMDP or privacy-focused datasets. 2) Implementing a regularization term that discourages activations in these subspaces during fine-tuning or continued pre-training. 3) Experimentally validating the approach by training LLMs and measuring trade-offs on safety benchmarks (MMDT-style evaluations), privacy probes (LLM-PBE-style), and efficiency metrics (parameter utility, inference speed).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training paradigm that directly optimizes internal representations for safety and efficiency. It will yield a publicly available benchmark suite for representation-based auditing during training and demonstrate concrete Pareto improvements in the alignment-efficiency frontier for medium-scale language models.
proposals.novelty: While representation analysis is used for post-hoc auditing, its direct integration as a differentiable training signal for joint alignment and efficiency optimization is unexplored. This moves beyond unlearning or output-level constraints to shape the model's internal computational pathways proactively.

proposals.title: Beyond Softmax: A Benchmark for Evaluating Confidence and Trustworthiness in LLMs
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often use softmax probabilities as a proxy for confidence, but this is known to be poorly calibrated, especially on out-of-distribution or adversarial inputs. This creates significant risks for trustworthiness in high-stakes applications, as models can appear highly confident while being factually incorrect or manipulated. A systematic evaluation of alternative confidence measures is needed to improve safety and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that confidence metrics derived from model internals (e.g., attention entropy, layer consistency) and post-hoc methods (e.g., ensemble or perturbation-based measures) will provide more robust and reliable indicators of LLM trustworthiness than softmax probabilities alone.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark dataset mixing in-distribution, out-of-distribution, and adversarially perturbed prompts. We will implement and evaluate a suite of non-softmax confidence measures, including: (1) internal consistency metrics across layers and attention heads, (2) prediction depth based on early-exit strategies, (3) semantic entropy from multiple generations, and (4) metrics from Monte Carlo dropout. We will assess these measures against ground-truth correctness and adversarial robustness using the evaluation frameworks established in prior work like MMDT and WMDP.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a standardized benchmark for evaluating LLM confidence proxies, moving the field beyond softmax reliance. It will deliver concrete guidelines and open-source tools for developers to implement more trustworthy confidence scoring in deployed systems, directly contributing to AI safety.
proposals.novelty: While individual alternative confidence measures exist, this proposal systematically benchmarks them in a unified trustworthiness evaluation framework tailored to LLMs, directly addressing a core gap in safety evaluation highlighted by Hendrycks' prior research on risks and benchmarks.

proposals.title: Adversarial Stress-Testing via Detection-Inversion: Generating Hard Cases to Improve LLM Trustworthiness Evaluations
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for evaluating LLM trustworthiness (e.g., for privacy, safety, or misuse) rely on static, hand-crafted datasets. These can quickly become obsolete, failing to expose novel failure modes or sophisticated adversarial strategies. To build robust safeguards, we need a method to proactively generate challenging, misclassified, or out-of-distribution (OOD) examples that stress-test detection mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting existing detection criteria—using them as objectives for a generative model—we can systematically produce inputs that are misclassified as safe or that appear OOD, thereby revealing critical weaknesses in current trustworthiness evaluations.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, we formalize the inversion of a target detector (e.g., a privacy classifier from LLM-PBE or a safety scorer from MMDT) as an optimization objective. Second, we employ a controllable text generation model (e.g., a fine-tuned LLM or a gradient-based method using Gumbel-Softmax relaxation) to produce text sequences that maximize the probability of a target misclassification (e.g., a privacy-violating prompt classified as benign). We will validate the method by applying it to detectors from established benchmarks like WMDP and MMDT.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, automated tool for creating next-generation stress-test datasets, moving evaluation from static benchmarking to dynamic adversarial probing. It will directly contribute to the field of AI safety by identifying blind spots in current trustworthiness metrics and enabling the creation of more robust detection systems through adversarial training.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial example generation exists in computer vision, its application to inverting complex, multi-faceted trustworthiness detectors for LLMs is novel. This approach flips the traditional evaluation paradigm from passive measurement to active, criterion-driven exploration of failure boundaries.

proposals.title: The Trustworthy Agent Frontier: Benchmarking and Detecting Out-of-Distribution Failures in Embodied AI
proposals.motivation: As AI agents move from controlled benchmarks to open-world deployment in robotics, autonomous vehicles, and complex simulations, they face novel, unpredictable environments. Pre-2026 safety research has focused on OOD detection in static data modalities (vision, NLP), leaving a critical gap in sequential, embodied decision-making domains where failures can have physical consequences. This work addresses the need for systematic evaluation of agent trustworthiness under distribution shift.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that existing OOD and misclassification detection techniques, when adapted to the temporal and state-action structure of embodied agents, can reliably identify failure-prone scenarios, and that a novel benchmark can expose significant vulnerabilities not captured by standard performance metrics.
proposals.method: We will construct the Embodied Agent Distribution Shift (EADS) benchmark, comprising diverse simulation environments (e.g., Habitat, CARLA, WebShop) with curated OOD test splits involving novel objects, unexpected physics, or adversarial goal hijacking. We will adapt and evaluate a suite of detection methods, including confidence-based (e.g., softmax entropy, Monte Carlo Dropout), distance-based (Mahalanobis distance in latent state-action space), and prediction-based (reconstruction error from a world model) approaches on top of popular agent architectures (e.g., Transformer-based policies, diffusion planners).
proposals.contribution: This will yield the first systematic benchmark for OOD detection in embodied AI, a taxonomy of agent failure modes under distribution shift, and a comparative analysis of detection efficacy. The resulting tools will provide a crucial safety layer for deploying agents in the real world, directly extending Hendrycks' work on AI safety evaluation into the dynamic, interactive domain.
proposals.novelty: While OOD detection is well-studied for classification tasks, its application to the sequential, closed-loop decision-making of embodied agents is largely unexplored. This proposal moves beyond static data to evaluate trustworthiness in interactive environments where an agent's actions can compound errors over time.

proposals.title: The Adversarial Benchmark for Trustworthy AI (ABTAI): A Dynamic Testbed for Evaluating Robustness in Language and Multimodal Models
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for evaluating AI safety, trustworthiness, and privacy (e.g., MMLU, WMDP) are often static and can be overfitted, leading to a false sense of security. As models rapidly advance, detection methods for malicious use, privacy leakage, and harmful outputs must be stress-tested against more sophisticated, adaptive adversaries. This gap limits our ability to predict real-world failure modes and certify model robustness before deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a benchmark composed of dynamic, multi-turn adversarial challenges and procedurally generated tasks will reveal significant, previously undetected vulnerabilities in state-of-the-art language and multimodal models, providing a more accurate and demanding measure of their true trustworthiness.
proposals.method: We will construct ABTAI by extending the WMDP and MMDT frameworks with three novel components: (1) An adversarial task generator that uses LLM agents (e.g., based on GPT-4 or Claude) to iteratively create and refine harmful prompts, jailbreaks, and privacy extraction probes against a target model. (2) A multi-modal 'red teaming' module that tests for cross-modal consistency failures and safety violations in vision-language tasks. (3) A scoring system that measures not just binary failure/success but the resilience and adaptability of a model's safeguards over repeated, escalating adversarial interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a rigorous, open-source benchmark that pushes evaluation beyond static question-answering. It will establish a new standard for measuring progressive robustness, directly informing the development of more resilient alignment and unlearning techniques, such as those explored in WMDP.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing static benchmarks, ABTAI integrates adaptive, multi-turn adversarial generation directly into the evaluation loop, creating a moving target that better simulates real-world deployment pressures. It uniquely combines red-teaming agents, procedural generation, and cross-modal attacks into a unified framework.

proposals.title: Anomaly Detection in Activation Space: A Classic Approach to Out-of-Distribution Detection for LLM Trustworthiness
proposals.motivation: As large language models (LLMs) are deployed in high-stakes and open-world environments, their tendency to produce confident but incorrect outputs on out-of-distribution (OOD) or anomalous inputs is a critical safety risk. Current OOD detection methods for neural networks are often complex, model-specific, or computationally expensive, limiting their practical application for real-time trustworthiness evaluation. A simple, robust, and generalizable OOD detector is needed to flag unreliable model behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that classic, non-parametric anomaly detection algorithms applied to the internal activation patterns of a frozen LLM can effectively and efficiently identify OOD inputs, matching or exceeding the performance of more complex contemporary methods.
proposals.method: We will extract activation vectors from key intermediate layers of a pre-trained, frozen LLM (e.g., LLaMA-2) for a corpus of in-distribution text. We will then train lightweight, classic anomaly detection models—specifically Isolation Forests, One-Class SVM, and Local Outlier Factor (LOF)—on these activation representations. At inference, the anomaly score from these detectors will serve as the OOD score for a given input. We will evaluate this approach on established NLP OOD benchmarks, comparing against baselines like maximum softmax probability and Mahalanobis distance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, simple baseline for OOD detection in LLMs, decoupling the detection mechanism from model fine-tuning. It will contribute a systematic analysis of which activation layers and classic algorithms are most predictive of distributional shift, offering insights into the internal representations of uncertainty. The resulting tools can be integrated into safety evaluation benchmarks and monitoring systems for deployed agents.
proposals.novelty: While anomaly detection is a mature field, its direct application to frozen LLM activations for OOD text is underexplored. The novelty lies in rigorously benchmarking these classic, interpretable methods against modern neural approaches within Hendrycks' established framework of AI safety evaluation, offering a potentially more efficient and transparent alternative.

proposals.title: Ensemble Disagreement as a Robust Signal for Detecting LLM Hallucinations and Unsafe Outputs
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains, their propensity for confident hallucinations and subtle unsafe outputs remains a critical barrier to trustworthiness. Existing single-model detection methods, such as logit-based confidence scores, are often unreliable and can be gamed. Ensemble methods, proven in other ML fields for uncertainty quantification, are underexplored for systematic LLM safety evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that disagreement metrics within an ensemble of diverse, state-of-the-art LLMs provide a more robust and generalizable signal for detecting untrustworthy model outputs than confidence scores from any single model.
proposals.method: We will construct an ensemble of pre-2026 LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 2, Llama 2) with varied architectures and training data. On established safety and hallucination benchmarks (e.g., TruthfulQA, WMDP), we will generate outputs for each model and compute pairwise disagreement rates using semantic similarity metrics and exact match. We will then train a meta-classifier on features including ensemble disagreement entropy, average confidence variance, and individual model logits to predict output veracity and safety. The method will be compared against top single-model baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, benchmarked framework for using model ensembles for trustworthiness detection, providing a new state-of-the-art for identifying unreliable LLM outputs. It will also release a diagnostic dataset highlighting failure modes where ensemble disagreement is most predictive, guiding future model development and evaluation practices.
proposals.novelty: While ensembles are known for improving accuracy, their systematic application to quantify disagreement as a direct measure of LLM output trustworthiness—particularly for safety-critical errors—is novel. This approach moves beyond using ensembles merely for performance boosting to using their internal dynamics as a diagnostic tool.

proposals.title: Inverse Calibration: Training LLMs for Robust Confidence on Adversarially Hard-to-Detect Errors
proposals.motivation: Current LLM safety evaluations and trustworthiness benchmarks (e.g., Hendrycks' MMDT, WMDP) focus on detecting known failure modes. However, models often exhibit high confidence on novel, hard-to-detect errors that evade existing detection heuristics. This creates a critical gap in real-world deployment where overconfidence on undetected failures leads to catastrophic loss of trust.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adversarially generating examples where standard confidence metrics and safety detectors fail, and explicitly training models to lower their confidence on these 'inverse' examples, we can improve overall calibration robustness and reduce overconfidence on unseen errors.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage adversarial training framework. First, we use a red-team LLM agent, constrained by a suite of pre-2026 safety detectors (e.g., from MMDT, LLM-PBE), to generate outputs that are erroneous but bypass detection. Second, we integrate these 'detection-failure' examples into a training objective that combines standard cross-entropy loss with a confidence penalty loss, specifically targeting high-confidence predictions on this adversarial set. We will experiment with architectures like T5 and Llama, using techniques like Focal Loss and temperature scaling within the training loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating calibration under adversarial detection failures, 2) a training methodology for inverse calibration that complements existing safety fine-tuning, and 3) empirical evidence on the trade-offs between model helpfulness and calibrated uncertainty on hard errors.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard calibration methods that treat all errors equally, our approach specifically targets the long tail of errors that are most likely to be missed by contemporary safety tools, inverting the typical detection-driven training paradigm.

proposals.title: MMLU-Expert: A Benchmark and Training Framework for Achieving Expert-Level Accuracy Across All 57 MMLU Tasks
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, state-of-the-art language models still fall short of expert-level human performance on the Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) benchmark, particularly in specialized domains like law, medicine, and high-level STEM. This gap limits their trustworthiness and reliability for high-stakes applications. Achieving consistent expert-level accuracy is a critical milestone for assessing model capability and safety, as defined by Hendrycks' prior work on AI risk evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted training and evaluation framework, combining task-specific expert-guided data curation, strategic model scaling, and a novel verification mechanism, can enable a single model to surpass the 90% accuracy threshold (expert-level) across all 57 MMLU tasks, a feat not yet demonstrated.
proposals.method: We will first construct 'MMLU-Expert,' an augmented dataset with high-quality, expert-verified explanations and chain-of-thought rationales for each MMLU question, sourced via collaboration with domain specialists. We will then train a transformer-based LLM (e.g., a scaled-up decoder-only architecture) using a multi-stage curriculum: initial pretraining, intermediate task mixing, and final fine-tuning with expert data. A key novel component is the integration of a 'self-verification' agent module, inspired by recent work on LLM agents, which will critique and refine the model's own reasoning before final answer selection to reduce careless errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) the first model to achieve expert-level performance on the full MMLU suite, 2) the public MMLU-Expert dataset to spur further research, and 3) a reproducible training and verification framework for pushing models to extreme reliability on knowledge-intensive benchmarks. It directly advances the goal of creating more trustworthy and capable AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has improved MMLU scores via scaling or fine-tuning, our approach is novel in its systematic, expert-driven data augmentation specifically for the expert-level threshold and the integration of an agentic self-verification loop directly into the training objective for robustness.

proposals.title: The Self-Knowledge Benchmark (SKB): Measuring and Improving Calibration in Language Model Agents
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents in high-stakes, open-ended environments, their tendency towards overconfident and ungrounded outputs poses significant trustworthiness and safety risks. Existing calibration benchmarks primarily focus on static, knowledge-based question-answering, failing to capture the dynamic, multi-step reasoning and action-taking of modern agents. This gap limits our ability to assess and mitigate overconfidence in real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a suite of calibration-aware training objectives—including confidence scoring, abstention learning, and verifier-based refinement—into the agent training loop will significantly improve an LLM agent's self-knowledge, leading to better-calibrated confidence and more reliable abstention in uncertain, multi-step tasks.
proposals.method: We will first construct the Self-Knowledge Benchmark (SKB), a dynamic evaluation suite where agents must navigate tasks with embedded uncertainties, contradictions, and knowledge boundaries, requiring them to know when they don't know. Building on methods like those in the WMDP Benchmark, we will then develop a multi-stage training framework. This framework will fine-tune agents using a combination of (1) a confidence alignment loss that penalizes incorrect high-confidence predictions, (2) a selective prediction head trained for optimal abstention, and (3) a lightweight internal verifier module, inspired by debate and recursive reward modeling ideas, to critique the agent's own chain-of-thought for potential errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, challenging benchmark for evaluating calibration in agentic settings and a reproducible training methodology for improving self-knowledge. The resulting models will be more trustworthy, as they can signal uncertainty appropriately, reducing the risk of harmful, overconfident actions in autonomous systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on calibration targets classifiers or standard QA models, our approach uniquely focuses on the agentic paradigm, integrating calibration directly into action and reasoning loops. The SKB moves beyond static factuality to assess self-knowledge in dynamic, sequential decision-making contexts.

proposals.title: AdvBench-Adv: A Framework for Adversarial Robustness Evaluation of LLMs via Subtle Semantic Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Current LLM benchmarks often fail to expose brittle understanding, as models can exploit superficial patterns. As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains, their robustness to subtly rephrased or deceptive inputs is a critical, under-evaluated aspect of trustworthiness. This work directly addresses the gap in evaluating semantic robustness, a core safety concern pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit significant performance degradation when presented with adversarial question variants that preserve core semantics but alter surface form, and that systematic generation of these variants can reveal previously unmeasured failure modes.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that uses a combination of controlled rule-based transformations (e.g., negation insertion, synonym substitution with bias, passive/active voice shifts) and a generator LLM (e.g., GPT-4) guided by textual adversarial attack principles to create subtle variants of questions from established benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, HellaSwag). We will then evaluate a suite of frontier LLMs on this new adversarial dataset, analyzing failure consistency and categorizing vulnerability types.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a publicly available benchmark of adversarial semantic perturbations, AdvBench-Adv, enabling standardized measurement of LLM robustness. It will provide a diagnostic tool to identify specific linguistic and reasoning vulnerabilities, contributing directly to the evaluation and trustworthiness themes of Hendrycks' research.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial attacks exist in computer vision and some NLP tasks, our approach systematically applies them to general-purpose LLM question-answering to test *understanding robustness*, moving beyond traditional accuracy metrics. It integrates structured rule-based methods with LLM-driven generation specifically for creating semantically faithful yet misleading variants.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Anomaly Detection for Proactive Identification of Novel LLM Attack Vectors
proposals.motivation: Current safety evaluations and red-teaming for Large Language Models (LLMs) are largely reactive, relying on known harmful prompts and attack patterns.
