This leaves models vulnerable to novel, unforeseen adversarial attacks that exploit emergent capabilities or distributional shifts. A proactive, unsupervised detection system is needed to flag potentially harmful outputs from zero-day attacks not present in training or benchmark data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the latent representations and output distributions of LLMs, when subjected to novel adversarial attacks, will exhibit statistically detectable anomalies that can be identified using unsupervised learning, even when the specific attack vector is unknown.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage anomaly detection framework. First, we will collect a large corpus of normal model outputs across diverse, benign prompts. Second, we will train unsupervised detectors (e.g., Isolation Forests, One-Class SVMs, or autoencoder-based reconstruction models) on features extracted from these outputs, including embedding vectors, token probability distributions, and internal activation patterns. The system will be evaluated by injecting outputs from known attack benchmarks (e.g., AdvGLUE, HarmBench) and novel, handcrafted adversarial prompts into the test set to measure detection rates and false positives.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel benchmark for evaluating anomaly-based safety monitors and a publicly released detection toolkit. It will contribute a new methodology for proactive AI safety that complements existing supervised and red-teaming approaches, moving the field towards more robust, generalizable threat detection.
proposals.novelty: While anomaly detection is a classic technique, its application to identifying *novel* adversarial attacks on LLMs by analyzing model-internal states and output distributions is unexplored. This approach shifts the focus from cataloging known harmful inputs to characterizing the anomalous signatures of harmful model behavior itself.

proposals.title: Adversarial Alignment: Training LLMs for Robust Helpfulness Against Prompt Hijacking
proposals.motivation: Current safety training for large language models (LLMs) primarily focuses on refusal—teaching models to reject harmful requests. However, this creates a brittle defense; adversarial prompts can often circumvent these guardrails by hijacking ostensibly helpful responses to generate harmful content. A more robust approach is needed to ensure models remain helpful *and* safe even under sophisticated attacks, moving beyond simple refusal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying adversarial training to the *generation* of helpful responses, we can produce LLMs whose outputs are intrinsically more resistant to being hijacked for harmful purposes, thereby improving the robustness of alignment.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage adversarial training framework. First, we will use automated red-teaming agents, building on methods like those in the WMDP Benchmark, to generate adversarial prompts designed to hijack helpful responses. Second, we will employ a novel loss function that penalizes not just the generation of harmful content, but also the presence of latent features in the model's internal representations that correlate with hijackable outputs. This will involve fine-tuning a base model (e.g., Llama 2) using a combination of standard helpfulness data and these adversarial examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new benchmark for evaluating the robustness of helpfulness, 2) a novel adversarial training methodology for proactive alignment, and 3) empirical results demonstrating improved resistance to prompt hijacking compared to standard refusal-based training. It directly extends the trustworthiness evaluation themes of MMDT and LLM-PBE into the training domain.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training is known for safety, its application is typically for eliciting and refusing harmful requests. Our approach is novel in targeting the *helpful* response generation process itself to make it adversarially robust, an 'inverse' of the standard refusal paradigm.

proposals.title: CREF: A Generative Framework for Counterfactual Robustness Evaluation in Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for large language models (LLMs) and multimodal models often conflate high performance with genuine understanding, as models may exploit superficial statistical correlations in training data. This creates a significant trustworthiness gap, where models appear capable but fail under distribution shifts or adversarial perturbations that break these heuristics. Distinguishing abstract reasoning from pattern matching is a critical, unsolved problem for evaluating model safety and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative framework for creating systematic counterfactual variants of reasoning tasks can reliably expose this gap, and that a model's robustness score across these variants is a more accurate measure of its true reasoning capability than performance on standard benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will develop CREF, a framework that algorithmically generates benchmark tasks. For a given abstract rule (e.g., logical consistency, physical reasoning), we will use a combination of rule-based templates and a critic LLM (like GPT-4) to produce minimal, meaningful perturbations to surface features, problem framing, and irrelevant complexity. We will then evaluate target models (e.g., Llama 2, GPT-4, Claude) on both original and counterfactual instances. The core metric will be a robustness score, defined as the performance retention across the perturbed distribution, analyzed via failure mode clustering.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, scalable methodology for measuring the robustness of abstract reasoning in AI systems, moving beyond task-specific accuracy. It will produce a public benchmark of counterfactual reasoning tasks and an interpretable diagnostic tool for the AI safety and alignment communities, directly informing efforts to improve model trustworthiness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates adversarial examples or distribution shifts, CREF uniquely focuses on generating *counterfactuals* that directly target the invariant core of an abstract rule, providing a causal-style test for understanding. Its generative, principle-first approach is more systematic than curating naturalistic out-of-distribution data.

proposals.title: The Adversarial Consistency Benchmark (ACB): Diagnosing Genuine Reasoning in LLMs via Systematic Task Variation
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for large language models (LLMs) are susceptible to exploitation via memorization and sophisticated pattern matching, conflating recall with genuine understanding. This creates a critical trustworthiness gap, as models may fail unpredictably when faced with novel problem framings, despite strong benchmark performance. A method to rigorously distinguish memorization from understanding is essential for accurate capability assessment and safety evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: LLMs that have genuinely learned abstract reasoning principles will demonstrate consistent performance across adversarial task variants with altered surface features, while models relying on memorization will show significant performance degradation.
proposals.method: We will design a benchmark suite where core reasoning problems (e.g., logical deduction, mathematical reasoning, causal inference) are systematically transformed to create adversarial variants. Transformations will include syntactic paraphrasing, domain transfer, negation, and distribution shifts in problem parameters. We will evaluate a diverse set of LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Claude, Llama 2) on both original and transformed tasks. Performance consistency will be measured using a novel metric, the Adversarial Consistency Score (ACS), which penalizes high variance across semantically equivalent but superficially distinct problem instances.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more rigorous benchmark for evaluating true model understanding, moving beyond task-specific accuracy. It will deliver a diagnostic tool for the AI safety and alignment communities to identify models prone to memorization failures. The resulting benchmark and ACS metric will be released publicly to advance evaluation standards.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates adversarial examples to find failures, this proposal systematically generates *families* of transformed tasks to measure *consistency* as a diagnostic for understanding, drawing a direct analogy to cognitive psychology tests. This shifts the focus from single-point robustness to a statistical measure of conceptual grounding.

proposals.title: BIG-bench Dynamic: A Self-Improving, Adversarial Framework for Sustainable Capability Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale benchmarks like BIG-bench are static, causing models to overfit and rendering evaluations obsolete. This creates a false sense of security and fails to measure the true frontier of model capabilities and risks. A dynamic, adversarial evaluation system is needed to provide a sustainable and rigorous measurement of progress and trustworthiness.
proposals.hypothesis: An automated framework that adversarially generates novel, validated tasks from meta-templates can create a continuously evolving benchmark, preventing overfitting and providing a more accurate, forward-looking assessment of model capabilities and failure modes.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-component system: (1) A library of formal meta-templates defining task families (e.g., constraint satisfaction, recursive reasoning, deceptive instruction following) based on known LLM failure modes. (2) An adversarial synthesizer, built upon a strong frontier LLM (e.g., GPT-4 or Claude 3), that instantiates novel tasks from these templates. A separate validator model will filter for correctness and novelty. The system will output a unique, never-before-seen test suite for each major model release.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available framework for generating dynamic, adversarial benchmarks, moving the field beyond static evaluation. It will provide a more reliable measure of true capability progress and emergent risks. The methodology contributes directly to the literature on trustworthy AI evaluation and adversarial robustness.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates static adversarial datasets, this framework introduces a self-improving, generative system for benchmark creation. It formalizes the concept of meta-templates for task families and automates the entire pipeline from failure mode definition to validated test suite generation, ensuring perpetual novelty.

proposals.title: BIG-bench Hard++: A Diagnostic Suite for Probing Advanced Reasoning and Trustworthiness in Frontier Language Models
proposals.motivation: As language models scale, their capabilities and failure modes become more complex and opaque. Existing benchmarks like BIG-bench, while extensive, are being saturated by current models, failing to expose subtle risks in reasoning, long-horizon planning, and trustworthy behavior. A more challenging and diverse diagnostic suite is urgently needed to guide the safe development of future, more capable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending BIG-bench with tasks requiring multi-step reasoning, adversarial robustness, and explicit trustworthiness evaluations will reveal significant, previously unmeasured performance gaps and novel failure modes in state-of-the-art large language models.
proposals.method: We will construct a new benchmark, BIG-bench Hard++, by: 1) Developing novel tasks focused on causal reasoning, strategic deception detection, and multi-agent scenario planning, inspired by pre-2026 AI safety literature. 2) Implementing dynamic, adversarial evaluation protocols where task instances are generated or perturbed in response to a model's previous answers to test robustness. 3) Integrating explicit trustworthiness metrics from Hendrycks' prior work (e.g., MMDT, WMDP) into task scoring, evaluating for privacy leakage, harmful content generation, and resistance to malicious use prompts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a rigorous, open-source benchmark for diagnosing the next generation of models, moving beyond simple capability measurement to integrated safety and trustworthiness evaluation. It will establish concrete, difficult targets for model improvement and inform risk assessments for deployment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior benchmark expansions, this proposal uniquely integrates dynamic adversarial evaluation and explicit, pre-defined trustworthiness taxonomies into the core task design of a large-scale benchmark, creating a unified test for both capability and safety.

proposals.title: Introspect: End-to-End Training of Architecturally-Embedded Calibration for Trustworthy Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes, autonomous scenarios where accurate uncertainty estimation is critical for safety and trustworthiness. Current calibration methods are largely post-hoc, applied after training, making them brittle under distribution shift and inapplicable to intermediate reasoning steps in chain-of-thought (CoT). This limits their reliability for real-world decision-making where models must know when they are uncertain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an introspective calibration module, integrated directly into the transformer architecture and trained end-to-end with a joint accuracy-calibration objective on adversarially constructed data, will produce LLMs that maintain robust, well-calibrated confidence scores for both final answers and intermediate reasoning steps, even under distribution shift.
proposals.method: We will design a lightweight, introspective 'calibration head' that attaches to transformer layers, processing hidden states to output calibrated confidence scores. The model will be trained with a composite loss function combining standard cross-entropy with a calibration-aware term (e.g., an adaptation of Expected Calibration Error). Training data will include diverse, adversarially constructed splits (inspired by robustness benchmarks) to stress-test calibration. We will evaluate on held-out adversarial datasets and standard trustworthiness benchmarks (e.g., MMLU under corruption, TruthfulQA).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, architecturally-embedded method for introspective uncertainty quantification in LLMs, 2) a benchmark for evaluating calibration robustness in CoT reasoning under adversarial distribution shifts, and 3) a model capable of providing reliable, step-wise confidence estimates, advancing the deployment of LLMs in high-stakes autonomous systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike post-hoc calibration or separate confidence models, our approach integrates calibration as a native, trainable capability within the LLM architecture itself, enabling joint optimization for accuracy and uncertainty estimation across the entire reasoning chain, which is a non-obvious architectural shift from pre-2026 methods.

proposals.title: Confidence Mismatch: A Calibration-Centric Benchmark for Diagnosing and Mitigating Social Bias in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Existing research on LLM trustworthiness often treats bias mitigation and calibration (the alignment between a model's confidence and its accuracy) as separate problems. However, if models are systematically overconfident when generating harmful, stereotyped outputs or underconfident on safe, factual responses for certain demographics, this represents a critical, underexplored failure mode. Addressing this synergy is essential for developing models that are not only less biased but also more reliably self-aware about their limitations in sensitive contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that miscalibration in large language models is non-uniform and is significantly more severe for inputs and outputs related to socially sensitive concepts and demographic groups, and that explicitly targeting this miscalibration can lead to more precise and efficient bias mitigation.
proposals.method: We will first develop a benchmark suite extending existing bias datasets (e.g., BBQ, CrowS-Pairs) and trustworthiness evaluations (like those from Hendrycks' MMDT/WMDP work) to include per-instance confidence scoring via methods like sequence log-probability or Monte Carlo dropout. We will then introduce new metrics that jointly quantify bias severity and confidence error. For intervention, we will design and test calibration-aware fine-tuning algorithms, such as adding a regularization loss that penalizes overconfidence specifically on stereotyped model completions identified in the benchmark, and implement a selective generation mechanism that withholds low-confidence, potentially harmful outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel benchmark and metrics for evaluating the intersection of calibration and social bias, 2) new calibration-focused training and inference-time methods for bias mitigation, and 3) empirical analysis establishing the link between miscalibration and social harm, advancing the field beyond treating these issues in isolation.
proposals.novelty: While bias and calibration are well-studied independently, their explicit joint measurement and the development of interventions that use confidence scores to directly target biased behaviors represent a novel fusion of these research threads, moving beyond post-hoc debiasing or general calibration techniques.

proposals.title: Architectural Bias Divergence: Steering Foundation Models Away from Social Harms via Sparse and Hybrid Design
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) exhibit convergent performance and, worryingly, convergent social biases, as revealed by benchmarks like those from Hendrycks et al. (e.g., MMDT). This convergence limits the effectiveness of post-hoc debiasing, which often treats a monolithic, harmful representational space. To achieve more fundamental safety, we need to understand how model architecture itself shapes the acquisition and expression of bias during pre-training.
proposals.hypothesis: Deliberately inducing architectural divergence (e.g., via sparse activation, mixture-of-experts, or hybrid encoder-decoder designs) will systematically create variance in learned social bias profiles, providing a necessary and exploitable lever to architecturally steer models away from harmful representational spaces from the ground up.
proposals.method: We will train a suite of architecturally varied models (dense Transformer baseline, Switch Transformer, mixture-of-experts with varying expert counts, and a hybrid T5-style encoder-decoder) on identical data. We will then benchmark them against a comprehensive, taxonomy-grounded evaluation suite for social bias and harm, extending frameworks like the WMDP Benchmark to social domains. We will use causal mediation analysis and representational similarity analysis to trace how architectural components influence the propagation of biased associations through the network.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a map linking specific architectural choices to bias amplification/mitigation, 2) a novel benchmark for evaluating architectural bias divergence, and 3) concrete design principles for 'bias-steering' architectures, moving the field beyond post-hoc fixes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work benchmarks model outputs for bias, we propose to causally link internal architectural mechanisms to bias formation. Our approach treats architectural variance not as a nuisance for performance parity but as a primary resource for safety engineering.

proposals.title: BIG-bench Nano: A Benchmark and Training Framework for Achieving Complex Reasoning with Sub-100M Parameter Models
proposals.motivation: The drive towards ever-larger language models raises critical concerns about accessibility, environmental impact, and trustworthiness, as opacity and failure modes scale with parameters. Pre-2026, benchmarks like BIG-bench define tasks requiring complex reasoning, but success is largely confined to models with billions of parameters. This creates a gap: we lack proven methods to distill these capabilities into tiny, auditable models, which are crucial for safety-critical or resource-constrained deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that through targeted, task-specific architectural interventions and training regimes—inspired by model unlearning and privacy auditing techniques—key reasoning capabilities from BIG-bench can be instilled in models with under 100 million parameters, achieving performance competitive with vastly larger generalist models on a curated subset of tasks.
proposals.method: We will first curate a 'Nano-Bench' subset of BIG-bench tasks emphasizing reasoning chains (e.g., logical deduction, causal reasoning). We will then experiment with specialized, modular architectures, such as integrating external symbolic reasoning modules or mixture-of-experts layers within a small transformer backbone. Training will employ techniques like iterative distillation from large model reasoning traces, targeted data augmentation informed by privacy auditing methods (cf. LLM-PBE), and selective unlearning (cf. WMDP) to remove spurious correlations, forcing the model to rely on core task logic.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A public benchmark and framework for evaluating high-capacity reasoning in tiny models, 2) Novel architectures and training recipes that decouple parameter count from specific reasoning skill acquisition, and 3) Concrete evidence for the feasibility of highly efficient, specialized models, advancing the field towards more trustworthy and deployable AI.
proposals.novelty: While distillation and pruning exist, our approach is novel in directly targeting the complex, multi-step reasoning of BIG-bench with architectural hybrids and training informed by safety-oriented techniques (unlearning, privacy audits), moving beyond mere compression of large models.

proposals.title: DualBench: Automating the Generation of Challenging Evaluation Tasks via Recursive Language Model Self-Improvement
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for large language models (LLMs) quickly become obsolete as models improve, a phenomenon known as benchmark saturation. This creates a critical gap in our ability to accurately assess the capabilities and risks of state-of-the-art models. An automated, scalable method for generating novel, high-quality evaluation tasks is needed to keep pace with rapid model development and ensure robust safety and trustworthiness assessments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured, recursive process using a committee of current LLMs can generate, critique, and refine novel evaluation tasks that are more challenging and diverse than existing human-created benchmarks, effectively predicting and targeting the failure modes of next-generation models.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-agent framework where a 'Generator' LLM (e.g., GPT-4) proposes novel tasks and answer keys based on a seed taxonomy of vulnerabilities (e.g., reasoning flaws, harmful content generation). A separate 'Critic' LLM (e.g., Claude) evaluates these tasks for clarity, difficulty, and novelty. A 'Refiner' LLM then revises the tasks based on critique. This iterative loop, inspired by constitutional AI and debate principles, continues until a 'Validator' model (a held-out LLM) confirms the task is unsolvable by current models but valid. We will implement this using an agent-based architecture with programmatic execution for code-based tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel benchmark, DualBench, composed of AI-generated tasks, 2) an open-source framework for automated benchmark generation, and 3) empirical analysis showing that tasks generated by this method are more challenging for frontier models than existing human-curated benchmarks. This provides a sustainable method for evaluation in the face of rapid AI progress.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs have been used to augment datasets, the novelty lies in a closed-loop, multi-agent system designed explicitly for generating *evaluation* tasks, not training data, with a formalized process for recursive self-critique and difficulty validation targeted at future model capabilities.

proposals.title: The Robustness Transfer Gap: Benchmarking the Degradation of Adversarial and Distributional Robustness from SSL Pre-training to Supervised Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning (SSL) pre-training is known to produce robust representations, but the trustworthiness of a model is determined by its final, fine-tuned state. Prior to 2026, it is unclear whether robustness properties like adversarial resilience and out-of-distribution generalization transfer perfectly or degrade during task-specific fine-tuning. This gap is critical for safety-critical applications where downstream performance must be certifiably robust.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the robustness acquired during SSL pre-training does not transfer perfectly; it degrades significantly during supervised fine-tuning on a specific downstream task, creating a 'robustness transfer gap'.
proposals.method: We will adapt the WMDP benchmark methodology to create a controlled evaluation suite. Using Vision Transformers (ViTs) and ResNets, we will pre-train models with SSL objectives (e.g., DINO, MAE). We will then fine-tune them on supervised classification tasks (e.g., ImageNet-1k) and rigorously evaluate robustness pre- and post-fine-tuning using established adversarial attack benchmarks (AutoAttack) and distribution shift datasets (ImageNet-C, -R). We will analyze layer-wise representations to isolate where degradation occurs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic benchmark quantifying the robustness transfer gap for vision models, providing a clear metric for the trustworthiness erosion in fine-tuning. It will offer actionable insights for developing fine-tuning methods that preserve pre-training robustness, directly contributing to the safety and reliability of foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates robustness of either pre-trained or fine-tuned models in isolation, our proposal directly measures the *change* in robustness caused by fine-tuning, a causal relationship not yet benchmarked. This bridges the separate research threads of SSL robustness and downstream task trustworthiness.

proposals.title: Beyond the Supervised Ceiling: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Robustness of Self-Supervised Language Models
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in trustworthy AI has established supervised learning as a standard for model performance, but its robustness to distribution shifts, adversarial examples, and out-of-distribution data is often brittle. Self-supervised learning (SSL) has shown promise in areas like outlier detection, but a systematic, multi-metric evaluation is needed to determine if SSL can provide a fundamental robustness advantage across the board, which is critical for deploying safe and reliable language models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that self-supervised language models, by learning richer, more generalizable representations, can surpass the robustness of their supervised counterparts not only in outlier detection but across a comprehensive suite of trustworthiness metrics, including adversarial robustness, privacy leakage, and performance under distribution shift.
proposals.method: We will construct a rigorous benchmark, building on methods from MMDT and LLM-PBE, to compare SSL (e.g., contrastive learning, masked language modeling) and supervised models on identical architectures. The evaluation will extend beyond accuracy to include: 1) adversarial robustness using gradient-based attacks, 2) privacy via membership inference and data extraction tests, 3) calibration and uncertainty on out-of-domain data, and 4) performance on curated distribution shifts. We will analyze the learned representations to explain robustness gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence for or against SSL as a superior paradigm for model robustness, moving beyond isolated metrics. It will deliver a public benchmark for evaluating robust representation learning and offer concrete guidance on whether the field should prioritize SSL pretraining for safety-critical applications.
proposals.novelty: While SSL is known for good representations, the claim that it constitutes a strict upper robustness bound for supervised learning across all major trustworthiness axes is novel and counter to the prevailing use of supervised fine-tuning as the final, trusted step in model development.

proposals.title: SSL-NDO: Self-Supervised Learning for Near-Distribution Outlier Detection in Language Models
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes and safety-critical applications, detecting subtle, near-distribution failures—such as maliciously fine-tuned models, privacy-violating memorization, or agents executing harmful instructions—is paramount. Current outlier detection methods struggle with semantically coherent but dangerous outputs that are statistically close to the training distribution, creating a critical gap in trustworthiness evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that self-supervised learning objectives, specifically designed to amplify latent feature differences between in-distribution and near-distribution data, can create a representation space where challenging outliers are more linearly separable, significantly improving detection performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a contrastive SSL framework where positive pairs are generated via semantic-preserving augmentations of in-distribution text, while negative pairs are constructed using synthetically generated near-outliers (e.g., via controlled prompt injection or gradient-based perturbation). We will test a novel loss function combining a standard InfoNCE term with a margin-based penalty that explicitly enforces separation for these hard negatives. The method will be evaluated on the WMDP benchmark for malicious use and adapted privacy leakage datasets from LLM-PBE, comparing against baselines like Mahalanobis distance and standard contrastive learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, targeted SSL technique for improving outlier detection in LLMs, a corresponding benchmark suite for near-distribution anomalies, and empirical evidence linking specific SSL objectives to trustworthiness metrics. It will provide practitioners with a more robust tool for model auditing and safety evaluation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike generic SSL or outlier detection methods, our approach explicitly optimizes the representation space for the hardest cases—near-distribution outliers—by integrating their generation directly into the contrastive learning objective, a targeted strategy not explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: SSL-SUP: A Hybrid Training Benchmark for Robust and Trustworthy Language Models
proposals.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in high-stakes applications, their robustness to adversarial inputs, distribution shifts, and privacy-compromising data extraction is paramount. Pre-2026, supervised fine-tuning optimizes for narrow task performance but can reduce out-of-distribution robustness, while self-supervised learning (SSL) offers rich representations but lacks task-specific grounding. A systematic, empirical understanding of how to best combine these paradigms for holistic trustworthiness is missing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully scheduled hybrid training objective, integrating self-supervised and supervised losses, will produce language models that significantly outperform purely supervised or SSL models on a composite benchmark measuring robustness, privacy, and safety.
proposals.method: We will design a controlled training framework where models (e.g., LLaMA-2-7B) are trained with weighted combinations of next-token prediction (SSL) and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) losses, exploring strategies like sequential pre-training then fine-tuning, joint training with adaptive weighting, and multi-task learning. We will evaluate these models on a novel, unified benchmark extending Hendrycks' prior work, incorporating tests for adversarial robustness (e.g., AdvGLUE), privacy (inspired by LLM-PBE's membership inference and extraction attacks), and safety (using WMDP-style knowledge unlearning efficacy).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a public benchmark for evaluating hybrid training strategies on multi-faceted trustworthiness, 2) empirical guidelines and open-source code for optimal hybrid training schedules, and 3) new state-of-the-art baselines for robust model development that balance task accuracy with safety and privacy.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid training is known, its systematic application and evaluation for the specific goal of maximizing a composite trustworthiness score—encompassing safety, privacy, and robustness—against sophisticated, modern attacks is novel and underexplored.

ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that model scaling does not aid real-world distribution shift robustness.

ideas.title: Synthetic Data Augmentation via Adversarial Curriculum Learning
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational ecology for generating synthetic, plausible species habitat data to the problem of improving training data for coding challenge competence. In ecology, models generate synthetic environmental data points within the validated 'envelope' of known species occurrences, using adversarial methods to ensure realism. Adapted here, we propose a system that models the feature space of human-authored coding challenges (e.g., algorithmic tags, complexity, structure). A generator network, guided by an adversarial discriminator, creates novel coding problems that are synthetically valid—meaning they are solvable, have clear specifications, and test distinct competencies—yet are not mere duplicates of existing data. This adversarial curriculum learning approach would systematically expand and diversify the training dataset, specifically targeting rare or underrepresented problem types to improve model robustness and generalization on coding tasks.

ideas.title: Apply adversarial training principles to OE
ideas.description: Use adversarial example generation techniques to create challenging, informative outliers for exposure.
ideas.sourceTitle: Deep Anomaly Detection with Outlier Exposure

ideas.title: Apply anomaly detection to identify novel attacks
ideas.description: Use unsupervised anomaly detection on model outputs to flag potential novel harmful responses not in training data.
ideas.sourceTitle: HarmBench: A Standardized Evaluation Framework for Automated Red Teaming and Robust Refusal

ideas.title: Create fine-grained adversarial datasets within classes
ideas.description: Apply filtration within a single class to find subcategories that are particularly challenging.
ideas.sourceTitle: Natural Adversarial Examples

ideas.title: Counterfactual Robustness Evaluation: A Framework for Distinguishing Abstract Reasoning from Pattern Matching
ideas.description: This research designs a generative framework for creating benchmark tasks that test for counterfactual robustness, a necessary condition for true understanding. Each task is built around an abstract principle or rule, then systematically altered via minimal but meaningful perturbations to surface features, problem framing, or irrelevant complexity. Success requires applying the invariant core reasoning to these novel, adversarially constructed instances, which are designed to break shallow correlational heuristics. The system outputs both the tasks and an interpretable metric of robustness, providing a concrete method to measure the gap between performance on standard tasks and generalized understanding.

ideas.title: Adversarial Data Curriculum for Robust Coding Competence
ideas.description: This approach replaces static data curation with a dynamic, closed-loop system where a generator model synthesizes novel coding challenges aimed at the specific failure modes of a learner model. An evaluator model rigorously assesses the learner's solutions, identifying conceptual gaps and error patterns to guide the generator. This creates a continuously evolving, adversarial training curriculum that expands problem diversity and complexity in direct response to the learner's weaknesses, systematically improving robustness and generalization beyond historical challenge patterns.

ideas.title: Adversarial Task Design to Distinguish Understanding from Memorization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that success on standard benchmark tasks reflects true understanding in AI systems. Drawing an analogy from cognitive psychology's methods to distinguish recall from recognition, the adapted research idea proposes designing adversarial benchmark tasks. The analogous solution from psychology involves testing knowledge transfer to novel scenarios where simple pattern matching fails. Adapted to AI, this means creating benchmark suites where core reasoning problems are systematically re-framed with altered surface features, distributions, or presentation formats. A model's performance consistency across these adversarial variants would serve as a diagnostic for genuine reasoning versus sophisticated pattern matching and memorization, providing a more rigorous measure of true understanding.

ideas.title: Architecturally-Embedded, Introspective Calibration for Large Language Models
ideas.description: This research develops a new paradigm where calibration is not an external adjustment but an intrinsic capability built into the LM's architecture. We design a lightweight, introspective module that analyzes the model's internal state to produce well-calibrated confidence scores alongside its predictions, applicable to both final answers and intermediate reasoning steps. The model is trained end-to-end with a loss that jointly optimizes for accuracy and calibration across diverse, adversarially constructed data splits. This results in a system that maintains robust calibration under distribution shift and provides reliable uncertainty estimates for its own chain-of-thought, making it suitable for high-stakes, autonomous decision-making scenarios.

ideas.title: Calibration-Aware Bias Mitigation: Using Confidence Scores to Diagnose and Repair Social Harm in LLMs
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that miscalibration in large language models is not uniform but is systematically worse for inputs or outputs related to socially sensitive concepts, demographics, or harm categories. We propose to develop benchmarking suites and metrics that jointly measure bias severity and confidence error, identifying where overconfident wrong answers correlate with harmful stereotypes or where underconfidence in safe, factual responses indicates instability. The core synergy is the development of calibration-focused training and post-hoc interventions—such as confidence-regularized fine-tuning or selective generation based on calibrated uncertainty—that directly use the model's self-assessment to mitigate social harms more precisely and efficiently than standalone bias or calibration techniques.

ideas.title: Architectural Interventions for Social Bias Divergence
ideas.description: This research investigates whether novel neural architectures (e.g., sparse, mixture-of-experts, or hybrid models) can be designed to systematically diverge not only in performance but, more critically, in their learned social biases and failure modes. By benchmarking these architecturally distinct models against comprehensive social harm taxonomies, we can identify which architectural choices amplify or mitigate specific biases. This enables a new mitigation paradigm: instead of applying uniform post-hoc fixes to converged models, we can architecturally 'steer' the learning process away from harmful representational spaces from the ground up. The core hypothesis is that breaking performance convergence creates a necessary and exploitable variance in bias landscapes, providing the leverage needed for more fundamental and effective harm reduction.

It employs a two-component engine: a library of meta-templates defining task families (e.g., constraint satisfaction, recursive reasoning) and an adversarial synthesizer model that instantiates novel, validated tasks targeting known failure modes. The system automatically generates a never-before-seen test suite for each major model release, ensuring the benchmark is always probing the current frontier. This creates a sustainable, forward-looking measurement of capabilities rather than a historical record.

ideas.description: This research designs an adaptive benchmark system that moves beyond static concept inventories. It employs continuous analysis of learner interaction data (code, errors, discussions) to automatically detect and validate new, emerging programming misconceptions. The system dynamically generates and incorporates new diagnostic problems into the benchmark, ensuring it remains current with new languages, tools, and pedagogical contexts. This creates a living, evidence-based standard for evaluating both human understanding and AI code generation capabilities.

ideas.title: Architectural Divergence as a Probe for Genuine Understanding
ideas.description: This research proposes that if all model architectures converge to similar performance on current benchmarks, it may indicate the benchmarks are measuring surface-level pattern matching rather than robust understanding. To test this, we will design novel, constrained architectures (e.g., with enforced sparse, modular, or recurrent reasoning pathways) that are forced to solve problems in ways fundamentally different from standard dense transformers. Their performance will be evaluated not just on standard metrics, but on new diagnostics that measure generalization under distribution shifts, causal reasoning, and explainability. Divergence in performance and failure modes across these architectures will reveal the aspects of a task that require genuine reasoning versus those solvable by memorization, thereby pressure-testing both model design and benchmark validity simultaneously.

ideas.title: Efficient Lifelong Adaptation: Decoupling Capability from Scale and Data Recency
ideas.description: This research challenges the dual assumptions that capability requires massive scale and that performance is independent of data recency. It posits that the need for large-scale models may stem from their static nature, forcing them to encode all possible knowledge upfront. By developing methods for lightweight, continuous temporal updating—such as parameter-efficient fine-tuning, dynamic memory architectures, or selective data integration—we can study whether smaller models can achieve and sustain state-of-the-art performance through efficient lifelong learning. The core question is whether decoupling capability from sheer scale is possible when a model is endowed with mechanisms for efficient, ongoing temporal adaptation.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune-Inspired Training: A Dynamic Memory Framework for Temporal Data Drift
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that static training data is sufficient for long-term model performance by investigating the impact of continuous temporal updating. Drawing an analogy from immunology, specifically the vertebrate adaptive immune system's solution to evolving pathogens, we propose a novel machine learning framework. The analogous immune system uses a dual-layer memory: a long-term, stable memory of past infections and a short-term, adaptable process for current threats. We adapt this solution by designing a neural network architecture with a analogous dual-memory system—a fixed core for foundational knowledge and a rapidly updatable 'adaptive' module for recent data. The study will focus on mechanisms to balance and integrate these components, such as gated attention or dynamic routing, to enable continuous adaptation to data drift while preserving core task performance, effectively making the model's knowledge base temporally resilient.

ideas.title: Preventive Process Audits for Contestable AI System Design
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a framework for Preventive Bias Auditing, which intervenes in the upstream technical and social processes of AI system creation to prevent the encoding of social harms. The approach moves beyond post-hoc benchmarking by creating structured methods to audit and redesign critical phases like problem formulation, training data curation, and objective function design, integrating continuous feedback from affected communities and domain experts. It produces tools and protocols to make value-laden design choices explicit and contestable, enabling developers to identify and mitigate potential bias pathways before a system is built. The outcome is a robust, process-oriented methodology that addresses the root causes of systemic bias, leading to more equitable AI systems by design rather than by correction.

ideas.title: Surpassing the Human Ceiling via Ensemble Calibration and Disagreement Modeling
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that aggregate human rater performance is an ultimate ceiling for AI models on reasoning or knowledge tasks. It draws an analogy from weather forecasting, where the ceiling of individual expert forecasters was surpassed by ensemble methods and calibration against forecast distributions. The analogous solution involved aggregating multiple numerical models and systematically comparing them to the historical record of expert predictions and their disagreements. Adapted to AI research, the idea is to model the full distribution of human expert outputs—including their variances, disagreements, and systematic biases—rather than just targeting the average. By creating an AI ensemble calibrated to this distribution, the model could learn to identify tasks where human consensus is weak or prone to systematic error. The core hypothesis is that such a model could then strategically outperform the aggregate human benchmark by deviating in a principled, calibrated way in these areas of human uncertainty, thereby surpassing the perceived ceiling.

It studies how continuous data streams (e.g., API changes, evolving specifications, shifting real-world constraints) can be integrated to define and assess 'correctness' as a moving target. The core synergy is a system that autonomously identifies when code has become semantically incorrect or incomplete due to temporal drift, even if it still passes its original test suite, thereby fusing deep semantic evaluation with temporal adaptation.

ideas.title: A Compute-Matched Search for Architectures That Defy the Performance Similarity Hypothesis
ideas.description: This research rigorously tests the convergence hypothesis by performing a large-scale, controlled search for architectures that fundamentally outperform standard transformers under strict compute parity. We define a vast, structured search space encompassing radically different sparse, recurrent, and hybrid attention mechanisms, then train and evaluate candidates using an automated pipeline that precisely matches total training FLOPs, data, and tuning effort. The core innovation is a meta-learning framework that actively seeks performance divergence, not just improvement, by optimizing for metrics that explicitly penalize similarity to the transformer loss curve. Success is defined by discovering an architecture class that sustains a statistically significant performance gap across multiple domains even after exhaustive hyperparameter optimization of the baseline.

ideas.title: Breaking Architectural Convergence via Ecological Niche Construction
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that all model classes inevitably converge to similar performance. Using an analogy from evolutionary ecology, it posits that the observed convergence between dense and sparse transformers stems from them being optimized in the same, homogenized 'environment' of standard training objectives and datasets. The analogous solution is niche construction, where organisms create new ecological niches to enable divergence. Adapted to machine learning, the idea is to investigate architectural innovations not in isolation, but as part of a co-design process with novel, constructed training environments. By developing methods to actively design distinct 'performance niches'—through tailored objectives, synthetic data distributions, or evaluation paradigms—the research aims to break the competitive exclusion between architectures, allowing sparse and other non-standard transformers to evolve uniquely superior capabilities that dense models cannot efficiently match, thereby fundamentally altering the performance landscape.

ideas.title: Surpassing Human Consensus by Ground-Truth Decoupling and Expert Calibration Tracking
ideas.description: This research investigates whether models can surpass not just aggregate human ratings, but the underlying truth or optimal performance those ratings imperfectly estimate. It develops a methodology that first identifies tasks where verifiable ground truth can be established via code execution, simulation, or formal methods, using these to audit and calibrate both human raters and models. For tasks lacking direct verification, it employs a Bayesian weighting system that prioritizes judgments from raters proven to be well-calibrated and discerning on related verifiable tasks. The final benchmark evaluates models against this calibrated truth estimate, revealing whether they can identify and correct for systematic errors in human consensus.

ideas.title: Algorithmic Efficiency as a Counterweight to Scale in AI Capability Development
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the core assumption that scale is the primary driver of all AI capabilities by drawing an analogy from computational biology's breakthrough in protein folding. In that field, the intractable problem of structure prediction was solved not by brute-force computational scaling, but by algorithmic innovation (e.g., AlphaFold2) that leveraged domain knowledge and efficient search strategies. Adapted to AI, the idea is to pursue capability gains not through scale-first approaches, but by designing novel learning algorithms, architectures, and training regimes that build in specific inductive biases (for reasoning, causality, or compositionality). The aim is to discover 'algorithmic levers' that can unlock targeted capabilities with high efficiency, providing a counter-narrative and practical pathway to advanced AI that does not depend exponentially on scale.

ideas.title: Neurosymbolic Specification Synthesis for Grounded Code Correctness Evaluation
ideas.description: This research develops a system that first synthesizes a formal, executable specification from a natural language programming problem description using a combination of large language models and symbolic reasoning. This specification serves as an objective, machine-verifiable ground truth for correctness, moving beyond reliance on human-provided test cases. The system then uses this specification to generate a comprehensive test suite, including standard unit tests and adversarial property-based tests that probe for functional gaps and edge cases. Finally, it evaluates candidate code solutions by checking conformance against this synthesized specification, providing a rigorous and explainable measure of correctness that is directly derived from the problem's intent.

It presents tasks requiring the use of multiple provided code modules, but these modules are subjected to controlled semantic mutations or have formally specified, potentially conflicting constraints. The model must first analyze the modules to identify inconsistencies, missing dependencies, or mutated behaviors. It must then plan and generate a solution that corrects, adapts, or negotiates between these modules to produce a correctly functioning whole, thereby demonstrating deep understanding of module semantics and robust integration skills.

ideas.title: Conceptual Correctness Benchmarks: Using Misconception Inventories to Evaluate Beyond-Test-Case Robustness
ideas.description: This research synthesizes educational research on programming misconceptions with the goal of moving beyond test-case-based evaluation. It involves constructing benchmark problems where the primary evaluation metric is a model's ability to avoid generating code that exhibits specific, documented student misconceptions (e.g., value vs. reference confusion, off-by-one errors in loops). The benchmark's 'ground truth' is not a single correct output for a set of inputs, but a taxonomy of incorrect conceptual implementations that a robust model must avoid. This creates a more rigorous and pedagogically grounded method for assessing whether a model has truly learned a programming concept, as opposed to merely learning to pass a finite set of unit tests.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Against the Human Expert Ceiling on Complex Algorithmic Reasoning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a new evaluation paradigm that fuses the concept of a human performance ceiling with the difficulty of complex algorithmic problems. Instead of using aggregate human rater scores on simpler tasks, it establishes the ceiling by rigorously measuring the performance of expert software engineers and competitive programmers on the hardest, interview-level problems from datasets like APPS. The core study then evaluates state-of-the-art models against this expert-derived ceiling, analyzing not just if they exceed it, but the qualitative differences in errors, solution strategies, and reasoning paths between models and top human performers. This allows for a nuanced diagnosis of whether surpassing the ceiling represents genuine algorithmic reasoning mastery or a different, potentially brittle, form of problem-solving.

ideas.title: Formally Verified Continuous Program Synthesis for Evolving Environments
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how formal verification methods from program synthesis can be adapted to prove the correctness of code that is continuously updated by a learning system. It challenges the static training data paradigm by designing synthesis algorithms that integrate live, temporal data streams while maintaining verifiable safety and functional guarantees. The core synergy is developing new formalisms and proof-carrying update mechanisms that allow a synthesized program to evolve its behavior in response to new information without violating its core specifications. This enables autonomous systems that are both adaptable to real-world changes and certifiably correct over their entire operational lifetime.

ideas.title: Formal Verification for Code Correctness via Model Checking
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that passing provided unit tests fully defines code correctness by drawing an analogy from hardware design verification. In that field, model checking is used to formally verify that a circuit design satisfies high-level specifications for all possible inputs, going beyond specific testbench simulations. We adapt this solution by proposing an evaluation framework where code submissions are assessed not only against unit tests but also against a suite of formally specified properties (e.g., safety, liveness, invariant conditions). The core idea is to develop a metric that integrates the results from automated formal verification tools, providing a quantitative measure of how thoroughly the code's correctness has been proven, thus offering a more rigorous and general guarantee of correctness than test cases alone can provide.

ideas.title: Evaluating Model Diversity via Portfolio-Based Benchmarks
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a task has a single correct solution specification by drawing an analogy from ecology's measurement of biodiversity. In evolutionary biology, ecosystem health is evaluated not by a single optimal species but by the functional diversity of species in a portfolio and their collective resilience. Adapting this solution, we propose designing AI evaluation tasks where the goal is defined by a functional outcome, and models are assessed on the diversity of valid solution strategies they produce. This involves creating new metrics analogous to ecological indices—such as functional richness and divergence—to quantify the variety and dissimilarity of a model's reasoning pathways or algorithmic approaches, thereby benchmarking for robust and innovative problem-solving rather than convergence on a single prescribed method.

ideas.title: Multi-Dimensional Capability Manifold Inference for Predicting Model Scaling
ideas.description: This research develops a non-parametric, multi-dimensional framework to model how distinct model capabilities (e.g., reasoning, knowledge retrieval) scale non-uniformly with compute and parameters. It infers a low-dimensional 'capability manifold' for models and a complementary 'task demand manifold' from sparse performance benchmarks, using Gaussian processes to capture complex scaling laws. This allows for accurate prediction of performance on novel tasks and identification of which specific capabilities are bottlenecks at a given scale, moving beyond a single, misleading 'ability' score.

ideas.title: Invert the standalone code generation task
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standalone code generation task by challenging a model to debug, explain, or modify existing buggy code. Drawing an analogy from Clinical Medicine, where practitioners are trained to diagnose and correct flawed treatment plans rather than create them from scratch, we adapt the solution of 'differential diagnosis.' The adapted research idea presents a model with a flawed codebase (analogous to a patient chart containing a faulty plan) and tasks it with identifying the bugs (diagnosis), explaining their root causes (etiology), and proposing a corrected implementation (treatment plan). This evaluates the model's ability for critical reasoning, understanding of code semantics and intent, and its capacity for repair rather than just creation, challenging the assumption that generation is the primary or most telling measure of coding proficiency.

ideas.title: Adaptive Temperature Scaling via Ensemble Feedback, inspired by climate modeling
ideas.description: This idea addresses the poor calibration of large LMs by drawing an analogy from climate science, where multi-model ensembles are statistically post-processed to produce reliable forecasts. The analogous solution involves aggregating outputs from different models and applying non-uniform, data-driven corrections based on historical performance. Adapted to LM calibration, the research would develop techniques to treat a single large model's internal variations as an ensemble, using a calibration set to learn an adaptive temperature scaling function. This function would condition on internal uncertainty metrics (like variance across attention heads or layer activations) to apply context-specific calibration, moving beyond a single scalar parameter to improve calibration accuracy across diverse inputs and confidence levels.

ideas.title: IRT-Based Calibration of Code Generation Models for Real-World Software Engineering Constraints
ideas.description: This research applies Item Response Theory (IRT) to model the probabilistic relationship between a code generation model's 'ability' (a latent parameter derived from its scale/architecture), the 'difficulty' of a software engineering task, and the probability of the generated code satisfying a specific real-world constraint like runtime efficiency or memory usage. By fitting IRT models to performance data across a spectrum of model scales and constraint-defined tasks, we can create a calibration curve that predicts the required model scale to achieve a target probability of success for a given constraint difficulty. This moves beyond simple aggregate performance metrics, providing a principled, diagnostic tool for understanding which constraints are hardest for models to learn and how scaling laws differ for functional correctness versus non-functional software quality attributes.

ideas.title: Evaluating Algorithmic Diversity as a Path to Efficient Capability
ideas.description: This research direction proposes designing benchmark tasks where multiple distinct, valid solution algorithms exist, none of which rely primarily on model scale or brute-force data ingestion. The core hypothesis is that if diverse, efficient algorithms can be discovered and validated for a capability, it demonstrates that scale is not a fundamental driver for that capability. The synergy lies in using the requirement for solution diversity (Idea A) as a rigorous test to identify and substantiate claims about capabilities achievable through alternative, efficient methods (Idea B), moving beyond single examples to establish general principles of efficient capability acquisition.

ideas.title: Applying Stress-Testing and Failure Mode Analysis from Aerospace Engineering to LLM Evaluation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from aerospace engineering's use of stress-testing and failure mode analysis, this research idea proposes a new paradigm for evaluating LLMs on complex algorithmic tasks. Just as engineers subject components to extreme, non-standard conditions to find breaking points and design flaws, we should systematically subject models to the hardest, interview-level problems in the APPS dataset with the primary goal of cataloging specific, reproducible failure modes (e.g., logical fallacies on dynamic programming, mishandling of recursion base cases, off-by-one errors in loops). The adapted solution moves beyond reporting aggregate performance metrics to instead produce a diagnostic 'weakness map' that informs targeted improvements in model architecture, training, or reasoning frameworks.

ideas.title: Applying Item Response Theory to Calibrate LM Benchmark Difficulty and Discriminatory Power
ideas.description: This research adapts Item Response Theory (IRT) from educational and psychological testing to formally assess the validity and reliability of LM capability benchmarks. The analogous solution from psychometrics uses statistical models to estimate item (question) parameters like difficulty and discrimination, independent of the specific sample of test-takers. Adapted to this context, we treat benchmark tasks or prompts as test items and different LMs as examinees. By fitting IRT models to LM performance data, we can calibrate benchmark difficulty on a continuous scale, measure how well each task discriminates between high- and low-capability models, and identify flawed test items (e.g., those with high guessing probability). This enables the construction of more rigorous, efficient, and interpretable evaluation frameworks, placing LM capabilities and benchmark demands on a common, theory-grounded measurement scale.

ideas.title: Temporal Adaptation as a Substitute for Scale
ideas.description: This research challenges the dual assumptions that capability emergence requires massive scale and that static training data is sufficient. It posits that a model's capacity for efficient, continuous temporal updating—learning from a live, non-stationary data stream—could itself be a fundamental engine for acquiring new skills and knowledge, independent of parameter count. The core hypothesis is that temporal adaptation mechanisms (e.g., efficient online learning, memory-augmented architectures) can unlock capabilities traditionally associated with scaling laws. The goal is to discover alternative scaling laws where 'temporal scale' (experience over time) and adaptation efficiency supersede brute-force parameter scaling as the primary lever for capability growth.

ideas.title: The Capability Decomposition Framework: Isolating and Engineering Emergent Properties Without Pure Scale
ideas.description: This research program challenges the assumption that scale is the only or primary driver of capabilities by developing a systematic framework to decompose emergent abilities into their constituent parts. It designs controlled experiments to test whether specific algorithmic improvements (e.g., better training dynamics, novel architectures), data curation strategies, or hybrid symbolic-neuro approaches can induce capabilities observed in large models, but in smaller, more efficient systems. The goal is to identify and engineer the minimal sufficient conditions for advanced capabilities, providing a principled alternative to scaling alone.

ideas.title: Extend BIG-bench via a 'Stress Test' Framework from Computational Materials Science
ideas.description: This idea proposes expanding BIG-bench by drawing an analogy from computational materials science, where multi-scale stress tests are used to probe material failure under extreme, combined conditions. The analogous solution involves designing simulations that apply simultaneous, interacting stresses (tension, heat, etc.) to discover novel failure modes. Adapted to AI benchmarking, this means creating new 'capability stress suite' tasks that don't just test a single skill in isolation. Instead, they integrate multiple known model weaknesses—such as long-context dependency, logical negation, spatial reasoning, and counterfactual scenarios—into complex, multi-step probes. This approach would systematically explore the interaction of failure modes in larger future models, mapping their 'failure boundaries' and revealing emergent shortcomings that simpler, discrete tasks would not expose, thereby more accurately probing their true capabilities and robustness.

ideas.title: Cryo-EM Reconstruction for Code: Generating Specifications via Inverse Problem Solving
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem-solving approach from Cryo-Electron Microscopy (cryo-EM) in structural biology to the task of generating specifications from code. In cryo-EM, the 3D structure of a molecule is reconstructed from many noisy 2D projection images. Analogously, we propose treating a codebase as a set of 'implementation projections' of an underlying, abstract specification. The research would develop a benchmark and models that take multiple code snippets (potentially implementing the same function in different styles, with different noise/irrelevant details) and iteratively align, classify, and refine them to reconstruct a single, canonical natural language description (the '3D specification'). This shifts the paradigm from a one-to-one code-to-text translation to a many-to-one inverse reconstruction, potentially leading to more robust and intent-capturing specifications, similar to how cryo-EM software robustly finds structure amidst noise.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Interpretability-Augmented HCI for Failure Diagnosis
ideas.description: This approach fuses scalable human behavioral evaluation with granular model introspection. By instrumenting models to export internal state data (e.g., attention distributions, gradient flows) during interactive tasks, it creates a time-synchronized log of mechanistic activity. This log is then analyzed against annotated human interaction data (e.g., think-aloud transcripts, hesitation points) to identify specific computational subprocesses that correlate with user-perceived failures. The result is a diagnostic framework that pinpoints not just that a failure occurred, but which internal model components and representations were involved, enabling targeted interventions.

ideas.title: Adapt Psychometric Dose-Response Modeling for Neural Scaling Laws
ideas.description: The original idea proposes applying Item Response Theory (IRT) to model scaling. By analogy, pharmacology's dose-response modeling offers a parallel solution for relating an input (dose) to a probabilistic output (effect). The analogous solution uses sigmoidal models (e.g., Hill equation) to fit experimental data, extracting parameters for maximum effect, half-effective dose, and curve steepness. Adapted to AI research, we treat computational scale (e.g., training FLOPs, model parameters) as the 'dose' and task performance (e.g., accuracy, log-likelihood) as the 'response.' The adapted method fits a family of sigmoidal scaling curves per (task, model family) pair, explicitly parameterizing the task's 'scale sensitivity' and the model family's 'performance ceiling.' This provides a unified psychometric-like framework to predict performance across scales, compare task difficulty, and diagnose scaling bottlenecks, moving beyond simple power-law approximations.

ideas.title: Sparse vs. Dense Neural Architectures: A Phase Diagram Approach from Statistical Physics
ideas.description: This research adapts the phase diagram methodology from statistical physics to thoroughly compare sparse and dense neural architectures. The analogous problem in condensed matter physics—understanding how a system's macroscopic properties (like magnetization) change across a landscape of control parameters (like temperature)—is solved by empirical mapping to reveal distinct phases and transition boundaries. We propose to treat model scale, task complexity, and sparsity as control parameters, and performance metrics as order parameters. By conducting a large-scale, systematic empirical study across this multi-dimensional space, we aim to generate a 'phase diagram' for neural network architectures. This will identify the precise conditions (scales, tasks, sparsity types) under which sparse architectures provide significant benefits, characterize any sharp transitions in these benefits, and establish predictive scaling laws to guide architecture selection—moving beyond point comparisons to a comprehensive, predictive framework.

ideas.title: Cognitively-Staged Model Distillation for Efficient Code Generation
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel training paradigm for small-scale code generation models by explicitly structuring the training curriculum using frameworks from cognitive development (e.g., Piaget's stages). Instead of training on a flat dataset, the model is exposed to a progression of tasks that mirror how humans learn programming, from concrete operations (syntax manipulation) to formal operations (abstract algorithm design). This staged, curriculum-based approach allows a compact model to internalize foundational reasoning schemas efficiently, reducing the parameter count needed for competence. The outcome is a highly efficient model whose capabilities emerge from a developmentally-plausible learning trajectory rather than sheer scale.

ideas.title: Beam Search for Iterative Code Repair and Explanation
ideas.description: This research adapts beam search and other NMT decoding strategies to the task of code repair and explanation. Instead of generating code from scratch, the model's output space is defined as sequences of edit operations, natural language explanations, or localized fixes applied to a given buggy program. The beam search maintains multiple high-probability *repair trajectories*, allowing the system to explore and compare different debugging strategies (e.g., fixing a symptom vs. addressing a root cause) or generate ranked sets of explanations with varying detail. This enables a more robust, interpretable, and multi-hypothesis approach to code understanding and correction than standalone generation or simple ranking of complete programs.

ideas.title: Use causal inference to identify robust features

ideas.title: Live Code Maintenance via Iterative Test-Driven Refinement
ideas.description: This benchmark evaluates a model's ability to perform realistic software maintenance by iteratively modifying a working codebase to meet new, naturally-specified requirements. Starting from a minimal correct program and a request for a change or new feature, the model must propose code edits. After each edit, an automated test suite runs, and the test results (including any failures or errors) are fed back to the model for the next turn. The task measures not just final correctness, but the efficiency and robustness of the model's debugging and refinement process in a dynamic, feedback-driven loop that mirrors real developer workflows.

ideas.title: BIG-bench for Compositional Code Generation and Integration
ideas.description: This fused idea expands BIG-bench with a new suite of tasks focused on compositional code generation. Each task requires models to solve complex problems by building upon, modifying, or integrating code modules they generated in response to prior, simpler subtasks within the benchmark. This creates a multi-stage evaluation where success depends on the model's ability to maintain consistency, understand dependencies, and repurpose its own prior solutions across a diverse range of difficulty levels and domains, moving beyond independent problem-solving to assess emergent architectural and integrative reasoning.

ideas.title: Specialized Modular Architectures with Formal Guidance for Efficient, Correct Code Generation
ideas.description: This research investigates hybrid neural-symbolic architectures for code generation, where a small core model is augmented with specialized, lightweight modules for distinct programming sub-tasks. A controller dynamically composes these modules based on the programming intent, while a integrated symbolic verifier provides immediate feedback and constraints during generation to enforce correctness properties. This approach ensures robust logical reasoning and security awareness are maintained at a fraction of the parameter count of monolithic models, by design rather than by scaling down a single network.

ideas.title: Compiler Optimization for Neural Code Generation
ideas.description: This research adapts the RISC and compiler optimization paradigm from computer architecture to neural code generation. The analogous solution involves using a small, optimized set of simple instructions (RISC) paired with a sophisticated compiler, rather than a large set of complex instructions (CISC). The adapted idea proposes developing a highly efficient, smaller core neural model specialized for atomic code operations. This core model is paired with a separate, trainable 'neural compiler' module that learns to map high-level programming intents into optimized sequences of calls to the core model. The system's competence in code generation comes from the compiler module's learned decomposition and planning strategies, not from scaling the core model's parameters, thereby achieving efficiency through architectural separation of concerns.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Triage and Remediation to Social Bias Mitigation
ideas.description: This research seed expands by drawing an analogy from ecological restoration in Environmental Science. In that field, practitioners use broad assessments to triage damaged ecosystems and then apply targeted remediation strategies (e.g., phytoremediation, biocontrol) adapted from successful interventions in similar contexts. Adapted to the original idea of studying social bias and harm mitigation, this research would develop a methodology that first uses broad benchmarking to identify and prioritize the most critical 'hotspots' of AI-mediated social harm. It would then research and adapt specific mitigation 'remediation strategies' from proven solutions in analogous social-technical problems, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches to create a prioritized, context-aware framework for deploying the most effective interventions where the need is greatest.

ideas.title: Investigate why scaling fails for mathematical reasoning

ideas.title: Interactive Beam Search: Using HCI Protocols to Steer and Evaluate Code Generation Decoding
ideas.description: This research fuses machine translation's beam search with HCI evaluation to create an interactive, steerable code generation system. During generation, a developer's think-aloud feedback or explicit corrections are used to dynamically re-rank, prune, or expand the beam search's hypothesis space in real-time. This allows the model to recover from early errors and explore alternative syntactic or semantic paths aligned with the user's unstated intent. The result is a co-creative debugging and generation tool that makes the model's decision-making transparent and corrigible, fundamentally changing the search objective from finding the most probable output to finding the most satisfactory human-AI collaborative solution.

ideas.title: Study social bias and harm mitigation strategies

ideas.title: Canonical Solution Equivalence: Measuring Diversity in Reasoning Strategy Space
ideas.description: This research designs tasks with multiple valid solutions and develops a method to evaluate models based on the diversity of their core reasoning strategies, not just surface-level output. It introduces a formal specification layer that defines atomic reasoning steps and strategic axioms, allowing for the algorithmic clustering of model-generated solution traces into canonical equivalence classes. Diversity is then quantified by the entropy and coverage across these fundamental strategy classes, ensuring the metric captures meaningful cognitive variation. The final system provides a robust benchmark for assessing a model's ability to explore and employ genuinely distinct approaches to problem-solving.

ideas.title: Improve model connection of world knowledge to values
