ideas.title: A Foundational Theory of Measurement for Language Models: Adapting Psychometrics to Non-Stationary Systems
ideas.description: This research develops a foundational theory of measurement for language models, recognizing them as non-stationary, prompt-sensitive systems distinct from human test-takers. It adapts core psychometric concepts—construct validity, reliability, and generalizability—by designing experiments that systematically perturb evaluation contexts (e.g., instructions, formats) and model states (e.g., sampling parameters) to quantify measurement error. The outcome is a robust framework that produces interpretable, model-agnostic capability estimates, separating true skill from benchmark-specific artifacts and contamination.

ideas.title: Formally-Constrained Beam Search for Code Generation via Integrated Compiler Feedback
ideas.description: This approach redesigns neural machine translation beam search by integrating real-time formal constraints from a programming language's grammar and type system. During decoding, partial hypotheses are passed through an incremental parser and static analyzer, which assigns a correctness penalty to beams that contain syntax errors or type mismatches. This hybrid scoring function combines the neural model's likelihood with a formal correctness score, steering the search toward compilable programs. The result is a beam search that is explicitly optimized for semantic validity, not just sequence fluency, dramatically increasing the yield of executable code.

ideas.title: IRT-Informed Scaling Laws for Sparse vs. Dense Architectures
ideas.description: This research fuses architectural comparison with psychometric scaling theory by using Item Response Theory (IRT) to model how the 'ability' of sparse and dense models changes with scale across tasks of varying difficulty. The core innovation is to fit IRT parameters not just to model scale, but to architectural sparsity itself, treating sparsity as a latent variable influencing a model's probability of solving a task. This allows the derivation of scaling laws that predict, for a given target performance on a task of known difficulty, whether a sparse or dense architecture is more parameter- or compute-efficient. The outcome is a unified framework to guide architectural choice across the scaling continuum, quantifying the trade-offs between sparsity, scale, and task demands.

ideas.title: Predicting Emergent AI Capabilities via Phase Transition Models from Statistical Physics
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to predicting emergent macroscopic properties from statistical physics to the problem of forecasting new AI capabilities from scaling laws. In condensed matter physics, phase transition theory successfully models how quantitative changes (e.g., in temperature) lead to qualitative shifts in system behavior (e.g., becoming superconducting), using concepts like order parameters and critical exponents. The adapted idea treats AI model scale (parameters, compute, data) as analogous to thermodynamic variables and new capabilities as emergent 'phases.' By analyzing scaling trends, the research aims to identify metrics that act as order parameters and to model the 'critical scaling' near capability thresholds. This framework would systematically predict when and what kind of new qualitative capabilities (e.g., complex planning, novel skill composition) should discontinuously emerge from the continuous quantitative trends of increasing model scale, moving beyond simple performance extrapolation.

ideas.title: Adapting Cognitive Walkthroughs from Software Usability to Model Debugging
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Usability Engineering in Software Engineering, this idea adapts the 'Cognitive Walkthrough' method. In the analogous field, this method involves evaluators systematically simulating a user's problem-solving process at each step of interacting with software to find usability failures. The adapted solution applies this structured, step-by-step simulation to AI model analysis. Researchers would develop a protocol to walk through a model's inferred decision process (using interpretability tools and probing techniques) for a given input, identifying at which specific internal 'step' the model's reasoning fails. This transforms HCI-inspired evaluation from observing final-user interaction to systematically auditing internal model cognition, providing finer-grained diagnostics for model failure modes.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Fitness Evaluation for Generated Code via Pareto Front Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from Evolutionary Multi-Objective Optimization (EMO) in engineering design to the evaluation of AI-generated code. In EMO, competing design objectives are handled by identifying a Pareto front of optimal trade-offs, rather than collapsing metrics into a single score. The adapted idea proposes evaluating generated code by constructing a Pareto front across key software engineering constraints like runtime efficiency, memory usage, and adherence to code style. This framework would allow developers and systems to select from a set of 'non-dominated' code variants, each representing a different optimal balance of the constraints, providing a more nuanced and practically useful evaluation than a monolithic score.

ideas.title: Adaptive Reasoning Landscapes: A Dynamic, Diagnostic Framework for Task Progression
ideas.description: This system moves beyond a rigid, age-based developmental sequence by constructing a dynamic model of a learner's reasoning. It begins with a diagnostic module that profiles an individual's current reasoning strategies across multiple dimensions (e.g., relational complexity, analogical transfer, causal modeling). This profile is used to place the learner within a multi-path 'reasoning landscape' informed by diverse frameworks, including cognitive development, expertise acquisition, and domain learning. The system then generates and sequences tasks that specifically target the most malleable gaps or bottlenecks in the learner's profile, enabling personalized, non-linear progression towards expert reasoning patterns.

ideas.title: Developmental Debugging: A Cognitive Progression for Code Understanding and Repair
ideas.description: This research fuses cognitive development frameworks with code debugging tasks to create a structured progression of buggy code challenges. Inspired by stages like pre-operational, concrete operational, and formal operational reasoning, tasks are designed to require increasingly sophisticated mental models of code execution, state, and abstraction. This allows researchers to evaluate whether a model's debugging ability develops in a human-like sequence, struggling with abstract bugs before mastering concrete ones, or exhibits a flat, non-developmental competency. The result is a novel benchmark that diagnoses a model's conceptual understanding of programming, moving beyond binary correctness to chart its developmental profile in code reasoning.

ideas.title: Apply Protein Folding Heuristic Search to Code Generation
ideas.description: The original idea proposes transferring beam search techniques from neural machine translation to code generation. By analogy, the field of computational biology faces a similar massive combinatorial search problem in predicting a protein's 3D structure from its amino acid sequence. This is solved not with a simple beam search but with sophisticated heuristic strategies like Monte Carlo simulations and fragment assembly, guided by a physics-based energy function to prune and refine candidates. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to develop a novel decoding strategy for code generation that replaces or enhances standard beam search with a heuristic search process. This process would use a learned or rule-based 'correctness function'—analogous to a protein energy function—that scores code candidates on multiple axes (syntax, semantics, execution output). The search would then involve probabilistically sampling, mutating, and recombining code fragments, iteratively refining the population of candidates toward optimal solutions, thereby more effectively navigating the vast space of possible programs.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Multi-File Code Generation for Causal Architectural Reasoning
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a new benchmark for evaluating code generation models that focuses on multi-file software projects with controlled, novel architectural constraints. The core innovation is designing tasks that require causal reasoning about module interactions—such as introducing a new, unseen design pattern or a circular dependency that must be resolved—to separate models that truly understand software architecture from those that replicate common file structures. Success is measured not just by syntactic correctness or compilation, but by the model's ability to adapt its generated codebase to satisfy novel, reasoning-intensive requirements that cannot be solved by pattern matching alone.

ideas.title: Programming Misconception Diagnostics via Clinical Assessment Design
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the diagnostic framework from clinical medicine to the domain of programming education. In medicine, validated diagnostic instruments are built upon rigorous research into disease symptoms and patient misunderstandings. Analogously, we propose to design a benchmark set of programming problems—a 'Programming Concept Diagnostic Inventory'—based on foundational educational research into common student misconceptions. Each problem or problem series is crafted to function like a clinical test, where the specific error pattern produced by a student reliably points to a single, identified underlying misconception (e.g., misreading loop termination conditions versus misunderstanding loop variable mutation). This moves beyond simple correctness to enable precise, differential diagnosis of conceptual bugs, providing a powerful tool for educators and researchers to measure conceptual understanding and tailor interventions.

ideas.description: This system addresses the specification gap by first using a neurosymbolic model to translate a natural language prompt into a candidate formal specification, explicitly identifying and querying a user about ambiguities. It then employs program synthesis under this specification, using formal verification to prove the generated code's correctness relative to the inferred spec. An interactive loop allows the system to present counterexamples or behavioral mismatches to the user, refining both the specification and the code until convergence on a verified, intent-aligned program.

ideas.description: This system generates and evaluates code by explicitly modeling real-world engineering as a trade-off space defined by a user-specified or inferred context (e.g., target deployment environment, project phase, business priority). Instead of producing a single output, it creates a Pareto-optimal set of code variants, each excelling in different combinations of efficiency, memory, style, and other constraints like dependency footprint or readability. Evaluation includes dynamic scenario testing (e.g., varying input loads, hardware profiles) to ensure robustness. The final output is a comparative analysis of the trade-offs, enabling the engineer to make an informed, context-driven selection.

ideas.title: Micro-Knowledge Probes: Isolating Fundamental Programming Constructs via Minimalist Benchmarks
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cognitive psychology's use of minimal tasks to probe specific mental constructs, this research adapts the methodology to evaluate fundamental programming concepts. The analogous solution involves designing constrained, 'pure' tests like the Stroop task to isolate a cognitive process. Adapted to programming, the idea is to create a micro-benchmark suite consisting of minimalist, functionally identical coding problems (e.g., a simple aggregation). Each problem in the suite must be solved using only one prescribed basic construct (loops, recursion, etc.). The benchmark evaluates a developer's mastery by measuring strict adherence to the construct specification and the canonical efficiency of its implementation, thereby scaling down complexity to provide a precise, isolated assessment of core programming skill components, free from the confounding variables of larger system design.

ideas.title: Architecture-First, Specification-Constrained Multi-Module Code Generation
ideas.description: This approach introduces a mandatory architectural planning phase before any code is generated. The model first produces a structured specification (e.g., in a DSL or JSON schema) outlining modules, their exported and imported functions/data types, and interaction protocols. This specification acts as a single source of truth. The code generation for each file is then executed under the constraints of this spec, with cross-file consistency checks. The final output includes both the executable specification and the generated code, enabling validation of the system's architectural integrity and coherence.

ideas.title: Use Phylogenetic Sequence Analysis for Cognitive Task Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of phylogenetic sequence analysis from evolutionary biology to the design of reasoning tasks. In phylogenetics, the incremental changes in genetic sequences are used to reconstruct evolutionary trees. Analogously, we propose treating the development of reasoning capability as an accumulation of discrete, identifiable cognitive 'units'. By analyzing performance data across a diverse population of learners, computational models can infer the most probable dependency graph or acquisition sequence for these units. This data-driven 'cognitive phylogeny' then directly informs the creation of a structured, incremental progression of tasks, ensuring each step builds on logically or statistically prerequisite skills, moving beyond broad developmental stages to a fine-grained, evidence-based curriculum for reasoning.

In that field, complex biological systems are built by hierarchically assembling standardized, well-characterized parts (BioBricks) with defined interfaces into devices and systems. The adapted research idea is to create a framework for code generation where a model uses a library of verified 'code bricks'—modular components with strict functional and interface specifications—as building blocks. The model would follow assembly rules akin to genetic circuit design, selecting and composing these bricks at increasing levels of abstraction (function, class, module, project) to generate a coherent, multi-file codebase where module interactions are correct by construction, solving the scaling challenge through principled hierarchical assembly.

Performance is measured on a composite metric of construct selection appropriateness, implementation correctness, and runtime efficiency, providing a robust assessment of conceptual understanding applied in realistic scenarios.
